name: सांख्ययोग
teaching: Krishna's first and deepest answer: you grieve over what cannot die, and cling to what cannot stay. Act because action is yours; let go of results because they never were. The chapter ends with a portrait of the person who has actually absorbed this — steady, undisturbed, free. Within one chapter the Gita plants all three of its great seeds — the deathless Self, the right to work without claim on fruits, and the portrait of the steady sage. Everything after is the flowering of these.
questions: What are you currently treating as permanent that is, honestly, passing?
If the results of your work were guaranteed to remain unknown to you, how would you work?
The sthitaprajña withdraws the senses 'as a tortoise draws in its limbs.' What would that look like in one hour of your day?

verses.speaker: सञ्जय उवाच
verses.devanagari:
तं तथा कृपयाविष्टमश्रुपूर्णाकुलेक्षणम् ।
विषीदन्तमिदं वाक्यमुवाच मधुसूदनः॥
verses.transliteration:
taṃ tathā kṛpayāviṣṭamaśrupūrṇākulekṣaṇam
viṣīdantamidaṃ vākyamuvāca madhusūdanaḥ
verses.essence: To him thus overcome by pity, eyes blurred with tears, despairing, Madhusudana speaks.
verses.translation: To him who was thus overcome with pity and who was despondent, with eyes full of tears and agitated, Madhusudana (the destroyer of Madhu) or Krishna spoke these words.

anvaya.meaning: eyes filled with tears and agitated

verses.explanation: Sanjaya's camera lingers one moment on the wreckage: pity-possessed, tear-blind, sinking. Then a single shift of name changes everything — the one who now speaks is Madhusūdana, the demon-slayer. Through all of Arjuna's lament Krishna said nothing; he let the despair complete itself. Now that there is nothing left of Arjuna's certainty, the slayer turns to his real target: not Bhishma, not Drona, but the delusion itself.
verses.connection: Picks up Arjuna exactly as 1.47 left him — collapsed — and turns the floor over to Krishna for the first time.
verses.reflection: Let someone see your tears today without rushing to explain or end them.
verses.background: Sanjaya narrates the turn: to Arjuna sunk in tears and pity, Krishna speaks his first words of the dialogue — not consolation but a bracing rebuke, questioning how such unmanliness has come upon him at the very hour of crisis, and commanding him to stand.

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
कुतस्त्वा कश्मलमिदं विषमे समुपस्थितम् ।
अनार्यजुष्टमस्वर्ग्यमकीर्तिकरमर्जुन॥
verses.transliteration:
kutastvā kaśmalamidaṃ viṣame samupasthitam
anāryajuṣṭamasvargyamakīrtikaramarjuna
verses.essence: Whence comes this stain on you at the crisis hour — ignoble, closing heaven, breeding disgrace?
verses.translation: Whence is this perilous strait come upon thee, this dejection which is unworthy of you, disgraceful, and which will close the gates of heaven upon you, O Arjuna?
verses.explanation: The Gita's first divine word is a question — kutaḥ, *from where?* — and a hard one. Krishna offers no sympathy, no validation of the eloquent lament; he calls it kaśmalam, a stain, a contamination, and asks where it entered. The diagnosis is brutal because it reframes everything: Arjuna thinks he has seen the truth at last; Krishna treats his vision as a symptom. Three rapid adjectives strip the despair of nobility, of heaven, of honor — every value Arjuna claimed for it. Real teaching often begins by refusing the student's framing of his own problem.
verses.connection: Krishna's answer to the entire first chapter: not a counter-argument, but a question about the source of the arguments.
verses.reflection: When paralysis dresses as virtue, ask the blunt question Krishna asks: where did this come from, now?
verses.background: Sanjaya narrates the turn: to Arjuna sunk in tears and pity, Krishna speaks his first words of the dialogue — not consolation but a bracing rebuke, questioning how such unmanliness has come upon him at the very hour of crisis, and commanding him to stand.

verses.devanagari:
क्लैब्यं मा स्म गमः पार्थ नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते ।
क्षुद्रं हृदयदौर्बल्यं त्यक्त्वोत्तिष्ठ परन्तप॥
verses.transliteration:
klaibyaṃ mā sma gamaḥ pārtha naitattvayyupapadyate
kṣudraṃ hṛdayadaurbalyaṃ tyaktvottiṣṭha parantapa
verses.essence: Yield not to unmanliness, Partha — it does not become you. Cast off this petty weakness of heart and arise, scorcher of foes!
verses.translation: Yield not to impotence, O Arjuna, son of Pritha. It does not befit thee. Cast off this mean weakness of the heart! Stand up, O scorcher of the foes!
verses.explanation: Two imperatives, no philosophy yet: drop it, stand up. Krishna first tries the warrior's medicine — honor, shame, the names that summon identity: Pārtha, son of the heroic Pritha; Parantapa, scorcher of foes. He calls the grand cascade of chapter 1 kṣudram, *petty* — a small weakness wearing big arguments. It will not work; Arjuna's crisis is too deep for a pep talk, and Krishna surely knows it. But the attempt matters: it establishes that what Arjuna needs is not comfort but a reason to stand, and that no reason of the old kind remains.
verses.connection: Follows the diagnosis of 2.2 with a first prescription — the appeal to honor — which the next verse will show has already failed.
verses.reflection: Find the place where you have called weakness kindness — and stand up inside it, just one inch.
verses.background: Sanjaya narrates the turn: to Arjuna sunk in tears and pity, Krishna speaks his first words of the dialogue — not consolation but a bracing rebuke, questioning how such unmanliness has come upon him at the very hour of crisis, and commanding him to stand.

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
कथं भीष्ममहं सङ्ख्ये द्रोणं च मधुसूदन ।
इषुभिः प्रतियोत्स्यामि पूजार्हावरिसूदन॥
verses.transliteration:
kathaṃ bhīṣmamahaṃ saṅkhye droṇaṃ ca madhusūdana
iṣubhiḥ pratiyotsyāmi pūjārhāvarisūdana
verses.essence: How shall I fight Bhishma and Drona with arrows, Madhusudana — they who deserve my worship?
verses.translation: How, O Madhusudana, shall I fight in battle with arrows against Bhishma and Drona, who are fit to be worshipped, O destroyer of enemies?

anvaya.meaning: shall I fight back with arrows

verses.explanation: Arjuna answers the call to arms with the two names that make it impossible: Bhishma, Drona. The verse turns on one collision — pūjārhau, 'worthy of worship', against iṣubhiḥ, 'with arrows'. One offers flowers to such men; how does one offer arrowheads? And his choice of address is a quiet counter-thrust: you are Madhusūdana, Arisūdana — slayer of *demons*, of *enemies*. My targets are neither. The objection is real and Krishna never belittles it; he will answer it, but from a depth no one on the field yet suspects.
verses.connection: Direct rebuttal of 'arise' (2.3): standing up means shooting at his grandfather and his guru.
verses.reflection: Name the two 'unattackable' figures in your life — the ones your growth is afraid to confront.
verses.background: Arjuna answers back, still between the armies: how can he shoot arrows at Bhishma and Drona, worthy of his worship? He would rather beg than rule through their blood — and confesses that no kingdom, not even sovereignty of the gods, could dry the grief withering his senses.

verses.devanagari:
गुरूनहत्वा हि महानुभावान्
श्रेयो भोक्तुं भैक्ष्यमपीह लोके ।
हत्वार्थकामांस्तु गुरूनिहैव
भुञ्जीय भोगान् रुधिरप्रदिग्धान्॥
verses.transliteration:
gurūnahatvā hi mahānubhāvān śreyo bhoktuṃ bhaikṣyamapīha loke
hatvārthakāmāṃstu gurūnihaiva bhuñjīya bhogān rudhirapradigdhān
verses.essence: Better to live on alms in this world than slay these noble gurus — to kill them is to eat food smeared with their blood.
verses.translation: Better it is, indeed, in this world to accept alms than to slay the most noble teachers. But if I kill them, even in this world all my enjoyments of wealth and fulfilled desires will be stained with (their) blood.

anvaya.meaning: rather than slaying these noble gurus
even to eat alms-food in this world
but having slain the gurus, though they desire wealth

verses.explanation: For a kshatriya, living on bhikṣā — alms — is the deepest humiliation; Arjuna says he prefers it. Yet inside his protest a concession slips out: arthakāmān — these gurus *do* desire wealth, they chose Duryodhana's pay over righteousness, and Arjuna knows it. He honors them and indicts them in the same breath. The closing image is unforgettable: every pleasure won by their death would be rudhira-pradigdhān, blood-smeared. He is saying that some victories contaminate the victor — anticipating, without knowing it, the Gita's teaching that *how* one acts marks the actor more deeply than *what* one wins.
verses.connection: Escalates 2.4: not only can he not fight the gurus — even the fruits of such a fight would be inedible.
verses.reflection: Honor your teachers and still outgrow their conclusions; reverence was never meant to be a ceiling.
verses.background: Arjuna answers back, still between the armies: how can he shoot arrows at Bhishma and Drona, worthy of his worship? He would rather beg than rule through their blood — and confesses that no kingdom, not even sovereignty of the gods, could dry the grief withering his senses.

verses.devanagari:
न चैतद्विद्मः कतरन्नो गरीयो
यद्वा जयेम यदि वा नो जयेयुः ।
यानेव हत्वा न जिजीविषामस्-
तेऽवस्थिताः प्रमुखे धार्तराष्ट्राः॥
verses.transliteration:
na caitadvidmaḥ kataranno garīyo yadvā jayema yadi vā no jayeyuḥ
yāneva hatvā na jijīviṣāmaḥ te'vasthitāḥ pramukhe dhārtarāṣṭrāḥ
verses.essence: We do not even know which is better — that we conquer them or they conquer us. Those we would kill stand before us.
verses.translation: I can hardly tell which will be better, that we should coner them or that they should coner us. Even the sons of Dhritarashtra, after slaying whom we do not wish to live, stand facing us.

anvaya.meaning: we would not wish to live

verses.explanation: The warrior's fire of chapter 1 has burned down to honest ash: *we do not know*. Victory and defeat have become indistinguishable, since the victory he is offered requires killing the people who make life worth living. This is the precise shape of a true dilemma — not a hard choice between good and bad, but two paths that both end in loss. Arjuna has reached the most fertile state a student can reach: the end of his own resources. Confessed ignorance is the only soil in which teaching grows.
verses.connection: The certainty of 1.46 ('better to be slain') dissolves into open unknowing — one step from asking for help.
verses.reflection: Sit with one genuine dilemma today without forcing a verdict — let not-knowing be a clean state.
verses.background: Arjuna answers back, still between the armies: how can he shoot arrows at Bhishma and Drona, worthy of his worship? He would rather beg than rule through their blood — and confesses that no kingdom, not even sovereignty of the gods, could dry the grief withering his senses.

verses.devanagari:
कार्पण्यदोषोपहतस्वभावः
पृच्छामि त्वां धर्मसम्मूढचेताः ।
यच्छ्रेयः स्यान्निश्चितं ब्रूहि तन्मे
शिष्यस्तेऽहं शाधि मां त्वां प्रपन्नम्॥
verses.transliteration:
kārpaṇyadoṣopahatasvabhāvaḥ pṛcchāmi tvāṃ dharmasammūḍhacetāḥ
yacchreyaḥ syānniścitaṃ brūhi tanme śiṣyaste'haṃ śādhi māṃ tvāṃ prapannam
verses.essence: My nature crippled by pity, my mind confused about dharma, I ask you: tell me decisively what is truly good. I am your disciple — teach me, I have taken refuge in you.
verses.translation: My heart is overpowered by the taint of pity; my mind is confused as to duty. I ask Thee: Tell me decisively what is good for me. I am Thy disciple. Instruct me who has taken refuge in Thee.

anvaya.meaning: my nature overcome by the taint of faint-heartedness
who have taken refuge in you

verses.explanation: The hinge of the entire Gita. Three self-diagnoses — crippled nature, dharma-bewildered mind — and then the two sentences that change the relationship forever: śiṣyas te 'ham, I am your disciple; tvāṁ prapannam, I have taken refuge in you. Until this verse Krishna is a charioteer and friend, answering banter with banter; teaching cannot be imposed on an equal. By formally becoming a disciple, Arjuna *authorizes* the teaching. And note what he asks for: not consolation, not agreement — śreyas, the real good, niścitam, decisively. The Gita exists because one man, at the bottom of his collapse, asked the right question of the right person.
verses.connection: The unknowing of 2.6 ripens into surrender: the friend becomes a disciple, and the dialogue becomes a scripture.
verses.reflection: Say the rarest sentence aloud to someone wiser: I am confused about my duty — teach me.
verses.background: Arjuna answers back, still between the armies: how can he shoot arrows at Bhishma and Drona, worthy of his worship? He would rather beg than rule through their blood — and confesses that no kingdom, not even sovereignty of the gods, could dry the grief withering his senses.

verses.devanagari:
न हि प्रपश्यामि ममापनुद्याद्
यच्छोकमुच्छोषणमिन्द्रियाणाम् ।
अवाप्य भूमावसपत्नमृद्धं
राज्यं सुराणामपि चाधिपत्यम्॥
verses.transliteration:
na hi prapaśyāmi mamāpanudyād yacchokamucchoṣaṇamindriyāṇām
avāpya bhūmāvasapatnamṛddhaṃ rājyaṃ surāṇāmapi cādhipatyam
verses.essence: I see nothing that could dispel this grief that withers my senses — not unrivalled kingship of the earth, not lordship over the gods.
verses.translation: I do not see that it would remove this sorrow that burns up my senses, even if I should attain prosperous and unrivalled dominion on earth or lordship over the gods.

anvaya.meaning: an unrivalled prosperous kingdom on earth
or even sovereignty over the gods

verses.explanation: Arjuna shows Krishna the size of the wound so the medicine can be measured. His grief is ucchoṣaṇam indriyāṇām — a drought of the senses, drying him from inside — and he has already run the thought-experiment all the way up: earth's whole kingdom, no; the throne of the gods, no. Nothing *attainable* can cure this. The statement is more important than he knows: by exhausting every possible object, he has proven that the answer cannot be an object. Whatever heals him will have to be a different *kind* of thing — not something to get, but a way to see.
verses.connection: Justifies the surrender of 2.7: he has searched the entire scale of attainment and found no remedy in it.
verses.reflection: Check whether any promotion, success, or escape would actually touch your deepest current ache.
verses.background: Arjuna answers back, still between the armies: how can he shoot arrows at Bhishma and Drona, worthy of his worship? He would rather beg than rule through their blood — and confesses that no kingdom, not even sovereignty of the gods, could dry the grief withering his senses.

verses.speaker: सञ्जय उवाच
verses.devanagari:
एवमुक्त्वा हृषीकेशं गुडाकेशः परन्तप ।
न योत्स्य इति गोविन्दमुक्त्वा तूष्णीं बभूव ह॥
verses.transliteration:
evamuktvā hṛṣīkeśaṃ guḍākeśaḥ parantapaḥ
na yotsya iti govindamuktvā tūṣṇīṃ babhūva ha
verses.essence: Having spoken thus to Hrishikesha, Gudakesha says 'I will not fight,' O King — and falls silent.
verses.translation: Having spoken thus to Hrishikesha (the Lord of the senses), Arjuna (the coneror of sleep), the destroyer of foes, said to Krishna, "I will not fight" and became silent.
verses.explanation: Two words and then silence: na yotsye, I will not fight. After the floods of argument, the refusal itself is almost peaceful — and the silence after it (tūṣṇīṁ babhūva) is the most important silence in the poem. It is the silence of a man who has said everything, the empty page on which teaching can finally be written. Sanjaya's irony glints again in the names: Guḍākeśa, conqueror of sleep, declares his surrender to Hṛṣīkeśa, master of the senses. The one in charge of this chariot has just changed.
verses.connection: The discipleship of 2.7 is sealed by renouncing the last word: he states his position and stops talking.
verses.reflection: After declaring 'I will not', notice that you have not left — staying near the question is itself hope.
verses.background: Sanjaya marks the hinge of the whole poem: Arjuna declares 'I will not fight' and falls silent. And Krishna — between the two armies, facing the despondent archer — begins to smile, and speaks the words that open the teaching.

verses.devanagari:
तमुवाच हृषीकेशः प्रहसन्निव भारत ।
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये विषीदन्तमिदं वचः॥
verses.transliteration:
tamuvāca hṛṣīkeśaḥ prahasanniva bhārata
senayorubhayormadhye viṣīdantamidaṃ vacaḥ
verses.essence: To him despairing between the two armies, Hrishikesha — as if smiling — speaks these words.
verses.translation: To him who was despondent in the midst of the two armies, Krishna, as if smiling, O Bharata, spoke these words.
verses.explanation: One word before the teaching begins: prahasann iva — *as if* smiling. Not laughter at Arjuna's pain; a smile through it, the look of one who can see the whole road from above the fog. The smile says what no argument yet can: from where Krishna stands, this catastrophe is survivable, even luminous. And Sanjaya fixes the location one last time — between the two armies — because that is where every human being receives this teaching: in the middle, committed, unable to retreat into the past or see the way forward. The Gita is taught nowhere else.
verses.connection: Answers the silence of 2.9. Arjuna has emptied himself; Krishna's smile is the first drop of what will fill him.
verses.reflection: Recall a time someone smiled gently at your despair — and how the smile held more truth than pity.
verses.background: Sanjaya marks the hinge of the whole poem: Arjuna declares 'I will not fight' and falls silent. And Krishna — between the two armies, facing the despondent archer — begins to smile, and speaks the words that open the teaching.

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
अशोच्यानन्वशोचस्त्वं प्रज्ञावादांश्च भाषसे ।
गतासूनगतासूंश्च नानुशोचन्ति पण्डिताः॥
verses.transliteration:
aśocyānanvaśocastvaṃ prajñāvādāṃśca bhāṣase
gatāsūnagatāsūṃśca nānuśocanti paṇḍitāḥ
verses.essence: You grieve for those who need no grief, yet speak words of wisdom. The wise mourn neither the living nor the dead.
verses.translation: Thou hast grieved for those that should not be grieved for, yet thou speakest words of wisdom. The wise grieve neither for the living nor for the dead.

anvaya.meaning: those not to be grieved for
and you speak words of wisdom

verses.explanation: The teaching proper begins, and its first sentence carries a sting: you talk like a paṇḍit and grieve like a man who has understood nothing. Arjuna's arguments *sounded* wise — dharma, family, sin — but wisdom is not measured by vocabulary; it is measured by what one mourns. The truly wise grieve neither for the dead nor the living, because they no longer mistake what a person *is*. With this verse the Gita quietly relocates the entire battlefield: the problem is not Bhishma's body across the field, but Arjuna's idea of what Bhishma is. Everything until 2.30 unfolds from this single correction. Shankara begins his commentary proper at this verse, treating everything before it as stage-setting — for him, the Gita's first real word is this diagnosis of misplaced grief. The sentence's sting is in its balance: your sorrow is sincere, your words are wise, and the two have nothing to do with each other.
verses.connection: The first sentence of the promised teaching (2.10): it answers none of Arjuna's arguments and instead dissolves their shared premise.
verses.schools_note: Shankara's commentary famously begins in earnest at this verse, treating 2.11-2.30 as the Gita's jnana-core. The schools split on 'gatasun agatasun': Advaita hears the unreality of all loss; Vishishtadvaita hears the soul's indestructibility within real relations. All agree the wise grieve neither — they differ on why.
verses.reflection: Audit your griefs today: which mourn what is truly lost, and which mourn what cannot be lost?
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
न त्वेवाहं जातु नासं न त्वं नेमे जनाधिपाः ।
न चैव न भविष्यामः सर्वे वयमतः परम्॥
verses.transliteration:
na tvevāhaṃ jātu nāsaṃ na tvaṃ neme janādhipāḥ
na caiva na bhaviṣyāmaḥ sarve vayamataḥ param
verses.essence: Never was there a time when I was not, nor you, nor these kings — and never shall any of us cease to be.
verses.translation: Nor at any time indeed was I not, nor thou, nor these rulers of men, nor verily shall we ever cease to be hereafter.

anvaya.word: न तु एव अहं जातु न आसम्

anvaya.meaning: nor shall we ever cease to be

verses.explanation: Krishna's first metaphysical claim, delivered as a triple negative made of granite: there is no past in which we were not, no future in which we will not be. *I, you, these kings* — he sweeps the entire battlefield, both armies, into a single deathless company. If existence has no edges, then the sword that seems to end a person only ends an appearance. Arjuna's whole lament assumed that death subtracts someone from reality; Krishna begins by denying that reality can be subtracted from.
verses.connection: Grounds 2.11: the reason the wise do not mourn is that no one, ever, actually stops existing.
verses.schools_note: A famous fork in the tradition: Ramanuja and Madhva read the plural ('I, you, these kings') literally — souls are eternally many. Shankara reads the plurality as true only of embodied appearance, with one Self behind all. The verse carries both readings without strain.
verses.reflection: Look at someone and silently note: this one existed before this body and continues beyond it.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
देहिनोऽस्मिन्यथा देहे कौमारं यौवनं जरा ।
तथा देहान्तरप्राप्तिर्धीरस्तत्र न मुह्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
dehino'sminyathā dehe kaumāraṃ yauvanaṃ jarā
tathā dehāntaraprāptirdhīrastatra na muhyati
verses.essence: As the embodied one passes through childhood, youth and old age in this body, so it passes to another body. The steady are not deluded by this.
verses.translation: Just as in this body the embodied (soul) passes into childhood, youth and old age, so also does it pass into another body; the firm man does not grieve thereat.
verses.explanation: The first analogy, drawn from what everyone has already survived. The body of your childhood is gone — every cell replaced, every proportion changed — yet *you* did not die when it went. You have already crossed several bodies within this one life and called it growing. Krishna extends the line one segment further: death is one more such crossing, no more a terminus than your tenth birthday was. The dhīra — the steady one — is simply someone who follows this continuity without flinching at the largest gap. The argument is from experience, not scripture: you have already died to the child you were, and survived. Commentators call this the dehantara-prapti analogy — body-to-body passage made as undramatic as age-to-age passage. The dhira, the steady one, is simply whoever extends a familiar curve one segment further.
verses.connection: Makes 2.12 walkable: eternal existence demonstrated from the ordinary experience of outliving one's own childhood.
verses.reflection: You have already survived several selves — childhood, youth. Trust the continuity that survived them.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
मात्रास्पर्शास्तु कौन्तेय शीतोष्णसुखदुःखदाः ।
आगमापायिनोऽनित्यास्तांस्तितिक्षस्व भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
mātrāsparśāstu kaunteya śītoṣṇasukhaduḥkhadāḥ
āgamāpāyino'nityāstāṃstitikṣasva bhārata
verses.essence: Contacts of the senses, Kaunteya, give cold and heat, pleasure and pain. They come and go, impermanent — endure them, Bharata.
verses.translation: The contacts of the senses with the objects, O son of Kunti, which cause heat and cold, pleasure and pain, have a beginning and an end; they are impermanent; endure them bravely, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: but the contacts of sense with object

verses.explanation: From the deathless self, Krishna turns briefly to what *does* fluctuate: sensation and feeling. Cold-heat, pleasure-pain — these are mātrā-sparśāḥ, mere meetings of sense and object, weather passing through awareness. Their signature is āgamāpāyinaḥ: they arrive, they leave; arrival guarantees departure. The instruction is one word: titikṣasva — bear them, let them pass without building a self out of them. Arjuna's anguish, too, is weather. This is not a dismissal of his pain but its correct classification: real, intense, and *transient*. Matra-sparshah — 'touches of the measures': sensations are contacts, and contacts are by definition brief. The instruction titiksasva, 'endure them,' founds the whole Indian grammar of forbearance: not suppression, but outlasting — letting weather be weather until it proves itself weather.
verses.connection: Pre-empts the objection to 2.13: 'but the grief is unbearable.' Grief belongs to the class of things that pass.
verses.reflection: Next discomfort that arrives, whisper: this is weather, it comes and goes — and watch it obey.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
यं हि न व्यथयन्त्येते पुरुषं पुरुषर्षभ ।
समदुःखसुखं धीरं सोऽमृतत्वाय कल्पते॥
verses.transliteration:
yaṃ hi na vyathayantyete puruṣaṃ puruṣarṣabha
samaduḥkhasukhaṃ dhīraṃ so'mṛtatvāya kalpate
verses.essence: The person whom these do not torment, steady in pain and pleasure alike — that one is fit for immortality.
verses.translation: That firm man whom, surely, these afflict not, O chief among men, to whom pleasure and pain are the same, is fit for attaining immortality.
verses.explanation: Endurance is given its prize. The one whom sensations cannot vyathayanti — shake, pierce — has discovered something in himself that the weather does not touch; and only what the changing cannot touch can be amṛta, deathless. The logic is exact: equanimity is not stoic decoration, it is the *evidence* of immortality, the immortal part showing through. Sama-duḥkha-sukha — equal in pain and pleasure — appears here for the first time and will become one of the Gita's signature measurements of a free human being.
verses.connection: Completes 2.14: why endure? Because the capacity to stand unshaken is itself the doorway to the deathless of 2.12–13.
verses.reflection: Practice being the same person in praise and in pain for one whole hour today.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
नासतो विद्यते भावो नाभावो विद्यते सतः ।
उभयोरपि दृष्टोऽन्तस्त्वनयोस्तत्त्वदर्शिभिः॥
verses.transliteration:
nāsato vidyate bhāvo nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ
ubhayorapi dṛṣṭo'ntastvanayostattvadarśibhiḥ
verses.essence: The unreal never comes to be; the real never ceases to be. Seers of truth have seen the boundary of both.
verses.translation: The unreal hath no being; there is non-being of the real; the truth about both has been seen by the knowers of the Truth (or the seers of the Essence).

anvaya.meaning: of the unreal there is no coming-to-be
of the real there is no ceasing-to-be

verses.explanation: The chapter's philosophical summit, two laws in fourteen syllables: what is unreal (asat) never gains being; what is real (sat) never loses it. Everything Arjuna fears losing — bodies, relations, kingdoms — belongs to the order that was always arriving-and-departing; it never possessed permanent being to lose. What truly *is* cannot be threatened, by arrows or by anything. Grief, examined to its root, is always a confusion of these two columns — mourning the asat as if it were sat. The tattva-darśins are simply those who have stopped filing things in the wrong column. Commentators across schools treat this as the Gita's metaphysical axiom: sat cannot cease, asat cannot begin to truly be. Shankara reads the changing as mithya — dependently real, like the snake seen in a rope; Ramanuja as fully real but perishable modes. Both agree on the practical edge: invest only in what survives the audit.
verses.connection: Generalizes 2.14–15 into ontology: the passing and the permanent are two different orders of reality, and they never trade places.
verses.schools_note: Shankara reads this verse as the seed of the entire Advaita: only Brahman is sat, the world of change being neither real nor unreal. Ramanuja reads sat and asat as soul and body — both real, but of different grades. The verse is short enough to hold both empires.
verses.reflection: Of all that distresses you now, ask which part will exist in ten years — give attention accordingly.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. verses.devanagari:
अविनाशि तु तद्विद्धि येन सर्वमिदं ततम् ।
विनाशमव्ययस्यास्य न कश्चित्कर्तुमर्हति॥
verses.transliteration:
avināśi tu tadviddhi yena sarvamidaṃ tatam
vināśamavyayasyāsya na kaścitkartumarhati
verses.essence: Know that to be indestructible by which all this is pervaded. None can destroy the imperishable.
verses.translation: Know that to be indestructible, by Which all this is pervaded. None can cause the destruction of That, the Imperishable.
verses.explanation: What is the sat of the previous verse? Krishna gives its first positive description: it is *that by which all this is pervaded* — tatam, woven through, as space pervades every room without being any room's furniture. Pervading all, it has no outside from which an attack could come; weapons are *within* what they would have to destroy. The argument is quietly devastating to Arjuna's fear: you cannot make a hole in that which is the fabric.
verses.connection: Names the 'real' of 2.16 by its property: all-pervading, and therefore beyond the reach of any destroyer.
verses.reflection: Feel the one aliveness running through every person you meet today; you cannot injure that.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
अन्तवन्त इमे देहा नित्यस्योक्ताः शरीरिणः ।
अनाशिनोऽप्रमेयस्य तस्माद्युध्यस्व भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
antavanta ime dehā nityasyoktāḥ śarīriṇaḥ
anāśino'prameyasya tasmādyudhyasva bhārata
verses.essence: These bodies of the eternal embodied one — indestructible, immeasurable — are said to have an end. Therefore fight, Bharata.
verses.translation: These bodies of the embodied Self, Which is eternal, indestructible and immeasurable, are said to have an end. Therefore fight, O Arjuna.
verses.explanation: The two columns are now placed side by side: bodies — antavantaḥ, end-having, with expiry written into them; the embodied one — eternal, indestructible, immeasurable. And then the sentence that shocks every first reader: *therefore fight*. The 'therefore' does not mean killing is trivial; it means Arjuna's specific reason for refusing — that he would be *destroying* Bhishma and Drona — rests on a metaphysical error. He cannot destroy them; no one can. What the war decides is the fate of bodies and kingdoms, things already mortgaged to time. This is the teaching's first touch of the actual battlefield, and it lands deliberately early: the full ethics of *why* fight is still chapters away.
verses.connection: Draws the practical edge of 2.16–17 for the first time: from the indestructibility of the self, the command to act returns.
verses.reflection: Care for the body as a borrowed instrument — fully, lovingly, without confusing it for the musician.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. verses.devanagari:
य एनं वेत्ति हन्तारं यश्चैनं मन्यते हतम् ।
उभौ तौ न विजानीतो नायं हन्ति न हन्यते॥
verses.transliteration: ya enaṃ vetti hantāraṃ yaścainaṃ manyate hatam ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṃ hanti na hanyate
verses.essence: Who thinks it a slayer, and who thinks it slain — both fail to understand. It slays not, nor is it slain.
verses.translation: He who takes the Self to be the slayer and he who thinks It is slain, neither of them ï1knowsï1. It slays not, nor is It slain.
verses.explanation: A verse so exact it doubles as a koan (the Katha Upanishad says it almost verbatim, and Emerson rewrote it as 'If the red slayer think he slays...'). Two errors are struck down with one stroke: the victim's error — *I can be killed* — and the killer's error — *I kill*. Both arise from identifying the self with the body's transactions. Note carefully what this does and does not say: it does not absolve the *person* who pulls the trigger of responsibility — karma and dharma govern that, and the Gita will hold Arjuna fully to them. It says the *Self* was never party to the transaction at all.
verses.connection: Closes the loophole left by 2.18: not only can the self not be slain — it is not the slayer either. Agency, like mortality, belongs to another layer.
verses.reflection: Drop both stories today: I can destroy, and I can be destroyed. Act from what remains.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचिन्
नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः ।
अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो
न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे॥
verses.transliteration:
na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin nāyaṃ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ
ajo nityaḥ śāśvato'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre
verses.essence: It is never born and never dies; having been, it never ceases to be. Unborn, eternal, everlasting, ancient — it is not slain when the body is slain.
verses.translation: It is not born, nor does It ever die; after having been, It again ceases not to be; unborn, eternal, changeless and ancient, It is not killed when the body is killed.

anvaya.word: न अयं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः
anvaya.meaning: nor, having been, will it ever again cease to be

verses.explanation: The chapter's great bell-verse, tolling four names of the deathless: aja, unborn; nitya, eternal; śāśvata, everlasting; purāṇa, ancient-yet-ever-fresh. Birth and death are events — and events need a timeline; the self *is* the witness in which timelines appear, so no event can happen *to* it. The last line gives Arjuna the sentence he most needs, plain enough to carry into battle: it is not slain when the body is slain. Two different things are happening on a battlefield; only one of them can be done with arrows. The verse is the Katha Upanishad speaking inside the Gita, nearly word for word — the teaching's pedigree displayed. Na hanyate hanyamane sharire became, for centuries of the dying and the bereaved, the sentence held at the bedside: the body slain, the dweller unslain, the grammar itself a consolation.
verses.connection: States positively what 2.19 stated by negation — and supplies the formula that directly answers the dread of 1.28–31.
verses.schools_note: This verse arrives nearly verbatim from the Katha Upanishad (2.18) — the Gita signing its lineage. Advaita reads the unborn one as the single Self; Dvaita insists each soul is individually unborn and eternal, never merging. The pastoral use is shared: it is the verse spoken where bodies fail.
verses.reflection: Sit quietly and find the part of you that has never aged a single day.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
वेदाविनाशिनं नित्यं य एनमजमव्ययम् ।
कथं स पुरुषः पार्थ कं घातयति हन्ति कम्॥
verses.transliteration:
vedāvināśinaṃ nityaṃ ya enamajamavyayam
kathaṃ sa puruṣaḥ pārtha kaṃ ghātayati hanti kam
verses.essence: Knowing it indestructible, eternal, unborn, immutable — whom does such a person slay, Partha, whom cause to be slain?
verses.translation: Whosoever knows It to be indestructible, eternal, unborn and inexhaustible, how can that man slay, O Arjuna, or cause to be slain?

anvaya.meaning: whom does he cause to slay

verses.explanation: The knowledge is now turned into a question aimed straight at Arjuna's conscience: for one who *knows* this, where exactly is the victim? The verse covers both hands — hanti, slays directly; ghātayati, causes to slay — touching Arjuna's double fear of striking and of commanding strikes. The question is rhetorical at the level of the self, but it is preparing something further: if the deepest 'I' neither kills nor is killed, then action can perhaps be performed without the knot of *doership* — the thread Krishna will pick up when he turns from metaphysics to karma-yoga at 2.39.
verses.connection: Converts the doctrine of 2.20 into the second person: what, knowing this, do *you* think you would be doing to Bhishma?
verses.reflection: Knowing the indestructible in others, ask: whom could you still treat as disposable?
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
वासांसि जीर्णानि यथा विहाय
नवानि गृह्णाति नरोऽपराणि ।
तथा शरीराणि विहाय जीर्णा-
न्यन्यानि संयाति नवानि देही॥
verses.transliteration:
vāsāṃsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya navāni gṛhṇāti naro'parāṇi
tathā śarīrāṇi vihāya jīrṇāni anyāni saṃyāti navāni dehī
verses.essence: As a man casts off worn-out garments and takes new ones, so the embodied one casts off worn-out bodies and enters others that are new.
verses.translation: Just as a man casts off worn-out clothes and puts on new ones, so also the embodied Self casts off worn-out bodies and enters others which are new.
verses.explanation: The Gita's most beloved analogy, chosen for its precise emotional work. Clothes matter — they are fitted, worn daily, even loved — but no one stands at a wardrobe grieving a threadbare coat, because the wearer was never the garment. The image quietly performs a complete reversal of perspective: death, seen from the body's side, is destruction; seen from the self's side, it is *changing*. For Arjuna staring at Bhishma — a hundred years old, his body long since 'worn out' — the analogy is almost tender: what the arrows would take from the grandfather is the coat. Vasamsi jirnani — 'worn-out garments': the most quoted image of death in Indian literature. Commentators draw the corollaries gently: one does not mourn a wardrobe change, one does not fear the tailor, and the dignity of the wearer never depended on the cloth. Reincarnation here is not doctrine first but de-dramatization.
verses.connection: Gives 2.20–21 its unforgettable picture; abstraction becomes wardrobe, and dread loosens into familiarity.
verses.reflection: Think of death once today as a change of clothes — and notice what loosens in your chest.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. verses.devanagari:
नैनं छिन्दन्ति शस्त्राणि नैनं दहति पावकः ।
न चैनं क्लेदयन्त्यापो न शोषयति मारुतः॥
verses.transliteration:
nainaṃ chindanti śastrāṇi nainaṃ dahati pāvakaḥ
na cainaṃ kledayantyāpo na śoṣayati mārutaḥ
verses.essence: Weapons do not cut it, fire does not burn it, waters do not wet it, wind does not wither it.
verses.translation: Weapons cut It not, fire burns It not, water wets It not, wind dries It not.
verses.explanation: Krishna inventories every destroyer the ancient world knew — blade, flame, flood, gale: the four elements as four weapons — and reports each one's failure. The rhythm itself is the argument: na... na... na... na..., a fourfold door shutting. Why can nothing touch it? Because cutting, burning, wetting, drying are all *transactions between forms*, and the self is not a form among forms — it is the awareness in which forms appear. On a field bristling with literal weapons, Arjuna is told: every one of them is aimed at clothing.
verses.connection: Tests the 'wearer' of 2.22 against all four elemental destroyers — extending 'cannot be slain' to 'cannot be so much as scratched.'
verses.reflection: Name the deepest thing in you, and verify: no insult burned it, no loss drowned it.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
अच्छेद्योऽयमदाह्योऽयमक्लेद्योऽशोष्य एव च ।
नित्यः सर्वगतः स्थाणुरचलोऽयं सनातनः॥
verses.transliteration:
acchedyo'yamadāhyo'yamakledyo'śoṣya eva ca
nityaḥ sarvagataḥ sthāṇuracalo'yaṃ sanātanaḥ
verses.essence: Uncuttable, unburnable, unwettable, undryable — it is eternal, all-pervading, stable, immovable, everlasting.
verses.translation: This Self cannot be cut, burnt, wetted, nor dried up. It is eternal, all-pervading, stable, immovable and ancient.
verses.explanation: The four failures of 2.23 are recast as four impossibilities — not 'weapons do not cut it' but acchedya, *uncuttable*: invulnerability is its nature, not its luck. Then five positive names in a single exhale: eternal, all-pervading, stable, unmoving, everlasting. The verse works like liturgy; its sound is meant to settle into the body the way its meaning settles into the mind. Tradition treats these repetitions kindly: what the intellect accepts in one hearing, the heart accepts only after many. Krishna is not informing Arjuna anymore — he is *soaking* him.
verses.connection: Seals 2.23 by upgrading resistance into impossibility, and lets repetition do what argument alone cannot.
verses.reflection: Rest one minute in what cannot be cut, burned, wetted, or dried — it is reading these words.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
अव्यक्तोऽयमचिन्त्योऽयमविकार्योऽयमुच्यते ।
तस्मादेवं विदित्वैनं नानुशोचितुमर्हसि॥
verses.transliteration:
avyakto'yamacintyo'yamavikāryo'yamucyate
tasmādevaṃ viditvainaṃ nānuśocitumarhasi
verses.essence: Unmanifest, unthinkable, unchangeable — so it is called. Knowing it thus, you have no cause to grieve.
verses.translation: This (Self) is said to be unmanifested, unthinkable and unchangeable. Therefore, knowing This to be such, thou shouldst not grieve.
verses.explanation: Three final negations close the description: avyakta — the senses cannot display it; acintya — the mind cannot model it; avikārya — change cannot occur in it. Krishna is honest about the limits of his own discourse: the self cannot be captured by the very instruments now hearing about it, since eye and thought only handle objects, and this is the subject. 'Knowing it thus' therefore means something subtle — knowing it as the unknowable knower. And from that, the refrain lands for the first of three times: nānuśocitum arhasi — you have no right to this grief.
verses.connection: Concludes the portrait begun at 2.17 by sealing off the last exits — sense, thought, change — and converts the whole portrait into a verdict on grief.
verses.reflection: Stop grieving today for one thing the grief cannot reach, and feel the energy return.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
अथ चैनं नित्यजातं नित्यं वा मन्यसे मृतम् ।
तथापि त्वं महाबाहो नैवं शोचितुमर्हसि॥
verses.transliteration:
atha cainaṃ nityajātaṃ nityaṃ vā manyase mṛtam
tathāpi tvaṃ mahābāho naivaṃ śocitumarhasi
verses.essence: And even if you think it constantly born and constantly dying — even then, mighty-armed one, you ought not grieve.
verses.translation: But even if thou thinkest of It as being constantly born and constantly dying, even then, O mighty-armed, thou shouldst not grieve.
verses.explanation: A startling move for a scripture: Krishna momentarily *grants the opposite worldview*. Suppose there is no eternal self — suppose beings really are just born and really do just die, the materialist's universe. Even then, he says, your collapse does not follow. The teaching refuses to rest its whole weight on one metaphysical pillar; it will now argue from inside the skeptic's own premises. A teacher confident enough to arm the opposing view is a teacher aiming at something deeper than doctrine — at the structure of grief itself.
verses.connection: Anticipates Arjuna's possible unbelief in 2.12–25 and opens a second front: the argument from inevitability.
verses.reflection: Even by the bleakest worldview, your despair is optional — test your sorrow against its own logic.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
जातस्य हि ध्रुवो मृत्युर्ध्रुवं जन्म मृतस्य च ।
तस्मादपरिहार्येऽर्थे न त्वं शोचितुमर्हसि॥
verses.transliteration:
jātasya hi dhruvo mṛtyurdhruvaṃ janma mṛtasya ca
tasmādaparihārye'rthe na tvaṃ śocitumarhasi
verses.essence: For the born, death is certain; for the dead, birth is certain. Therefore, over the unavoidable, you should not grieve.
verses.translation: For certain is death for the born, and certain is birth for the dead; therefore, over the inevitable thou shouldst not grieve.

anvaya.meaning: and birth is certain for the dead

verses.explanation: The skeptic's-premise argument completes itself with two symmetries: born→death, certain; dead→birth, certain. The hinge word is aparihārya — that which cannot be averted by any effort whatsoever. Grief makes an implicit claim: *this should not be happening*. Against the genuinely unavoidable, that claim is incoherent — sorrow aimed at a law of nature is sorrow aimed at nothing. This is not coldness; it is the surgical separation of pain (inevitable) from the added torment of protesting the inevitable (optional). Bhishma and Drona, being born, were always already going to die; the only open question was the manner. Jatasya hi dhruvo mrityuh — the one certainty issued at birth. The tradition reads the verse as aimed at the grief that argues with structure: sorrow appropriate to a surprise is being spent on a schedule. Acceptance of the frame, it suggests, is what frees feeling to be clean inside it.
verses.connection: Completes 2.26: even granting that beings simply die, mourning the unavoidable is a category mistake.
verses.reflection: Accept one inevitability today fully — and watch how much fight in you was spent denying it.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
अव्यक्तादीनि भूतानि व्यक्तमध्यानि भारत ।
अव्यक्तनिधनान्येव तत्र का परिदेवना॥
verses.transliteration:
avyaktādīni bhūtāni vyaktamadhyāni bhārata
avyaktanidhanānyeva tatra kā paridevanā
verses.essence: Beings are unmanifest in their beginnings, manifest in the middle, unmanifest again in their ends. What is there to lament?
verses.translation: Beings are unmanifested in their beginning, manifested in their middle state, O Arjuna, and unmanifested again in their end. What is there to grieve about?

anvaya.meaning: what lamentation is there in that

verses.explanation: A wide, almost cosmological camera angle: every being is a brief visibility between two invisibilities. Before birth — unmanifest; after death — unmanifest; the 'middle' that we call a life is the only segment the senses ever report. We grieve as if the manifest stretch were the whole of the person and its end an annihilation; but if both shores are unseen, death returns a being to the same mystery it came from. The question kā paridevanā — *what is there to mourn?* — is gentler than it sounds: it asks whether we have ever actually seen the thing we believe was destroyed.
verses.connection: Reframes 2.27's inevitability spatially: life as a window of manifestation, with the same unknown on either side.
verses.reflection: All beings emerge from mystery and return to it; let that soften your grip on the middle part.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
आश्चर्यवत्पश्यति कश्चिदेन-
माश्चर्यवद्वदति तथैव चान्यः ।
आश्चर्यवच्चैनमन्यः शृणोति
श्रुत्वाप्येनं वेद न चैव कश्चित्॥
verses.transliteration:
āścaryavatpaśyati kaścidenam āścaryavadvadati tathaiva cānyaḥ
āścaryavaccainamanyaḥ śṛṇoti śrutvāpyenaṃ veda na caiva kaścit
verses.essence: One sees it as a wonder, another speaks of it as a wonder, another hears of it as a wonder — and even having heard, no one truly knows it.
verses.translation: One sees This (the Self) as a wonder; another speaks of It as a wonder; another hears of It as a wonder; yet having heard, none understands It at all.

anvaya.word: आश्चर्यवत् वदति तथा एव च अन्यः
anvaya.meaning: and likewise another speaks of it as a wonder
and another hears of it as a wonder

verses.explanation: After the dense argumentation, a sudden bow of humility — almost a sigh. The self is āścarya, a wonder, and the verse traces the whole chain of transmission: the rare one who sees, the one who can speak, the one who hears — and concedes that even hearing guarantees nothing. Krishna is describing this very conversation: he speaks as a wonder, Arjuna hears as a wonder, and understanding may still not dawn. Doctrine can be delivered; realization cannot. The verse protects the teaching from its own success — from the student who memorizes 'I am deathless' and mistakes the sentence for the seeing.
verses.connection: A pause for awe after 2.11–28: the truth just argued is not thereby transferred — knowledge of the self remains rare and wondrous.
verses.reflection: Approach the self today as a wonder, not a problem — astonishment is the correct first response.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
देही नित्यमवध्योऽयं देहे सर्वस्य भारत ।
तस्मात्सर्वाणि भूतानि न त्वं शोचितुमर्हसि॥
verses.transliteration:
dehī nityamavadhyo'yaṃ dehe sarvasya bhārata
tasmātsarvāṇi bhūtāni na tvaṃ śocitumarhasi
verses.essence: The embodied one in everyone's body is forever beyond slaying, Bharata. Therefore you should not grieve for any being.
verses.translation: This, the Indweller in the body of everyone, is ever indestructible, O Arjuna; therefore, thou shouldst not grieve for any creature.
verses.explanation: The metaphysical movement closes with a summary stamped sarvasya — *in everyone*. Not only Bhishma and Drona: the deathless dweller is in every body on both sides of the field, in Duryodhana as much as in Yudhishthira. The refrain 'you should not grieve' sounds its third and final time, now extended to all beings everywhere. The teaching has reached its widest circumference — and precisely here Krishna will pivot, because Arjuna does not live at the widest circumference. He lives in a chariot, with a bow, and a duty. The next verse descends from eternity to that chariot.
verses.connection: Seals the section begun at 2.11. Grief has been answered at the level of being; the level of *doing* now opens.
verses.reflection: Walk through one hostile room remembering: nothing here can wound the dweller in this body.
verses.background: Krishna's first instruction begins. With the armies frozen around the chariot, he addresses the root of Arjuna's grief rather than its occasion: the deathless, unborn, all-pervading Self that weapons cannot cut — the wisdom called Sankhya, delivered to a warrior who believed he was about to commit murder.

verses.devanagari:
स्वधर्ममपि चावेक्ष्य न विकम्पितुमर्हसि ।
धर्म्याद्धि युद्धाच्छ्रेयोऽन्यत्क्षत्रियस्य न विद्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
svadharmamapi cāvekṣya na vikampitumarhasi
dharmyāddhi yuddhācchreyo'nyatkṣatriyasya na vidyate
verses.essence: And looking to your own dharma, you should not waver — for a kshatriya, nothing is higher than a righteous war.
verses.translation: Further, having regard to thy duty, shouldst not waver, for there is nothing higher for a Kshatriya than a righteous war.

anvaya.meaning: and even considering your own dharma
does not exist for a kshatriya

verses.explanation: The word svadharma enters the Gita — one's *own* dharma, the duty woven into one's station and nature — and the teaching changes registers from being to role. Arjuna is not a generic soul; he is a kshatriya, society's appointed protector, and this war (Krishna calls it dharmya, righteous — the qualifier matters) is precisely the contingency his entire existence was shaped for. The argument is no longer 'no one really dies' but 'this is yours to do.' A surgeon who faints at the sight of the one wound only she can close has not transcended her role; she has deserted it.
verses.connection: The pivot from 2.30: having dissolved death as a reason to mourn, Krishna takes up duty as a reason to act.
verses.reflection: Ask what your station in life uniquely obligates you to do — then stop negotiating with it.
verses.background: The teaching descends from metaphysics to station: Krishna reminds Arjuna of his own dharma as a kshatriya — the open door of righteous battle, the infamy of retreat, what his peers and enemies will say — and seals it with the verse of equanimity that makes fighting sinless.

verses.devanagari:
यदृच्छया चोपपन्नं स्वर्गद्वारमपावृतम् ।
सुखिनः क्षत्रियाः पार्थ लभन्ते युद्धमीदृशम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yadṛcchayā copapannaṃ svargadvāramapāvṛtam
sukhinaḥ kṣatriyāḥ pārtha labhante yuddhamīdṛśam
verses.essence: Happy the kshatriyas, Partha, who find such a war — an open door to heaven, arrived unsought.
verses.translation: Happy are the Kshatriyas, O Arjuna! who are called upon to fight in such a battle that comes of itself as an open door to heaven.
verses.explanation: Krishna speaks for a moment in the pure idiom of the warrior code: a righteous war that arrives yadṛcchayā — unsought, by circumstance rather than ambition — is the kshatriya's rarest fortune, heaven's door standing open. The qualifier 'unsought' is load-bearing: Arjuna did not engineer this war; thirteen years of exile, an insulted queen, and every peace embassy refused brought it to his feet. He is not being asked to start a fire, only not to flee the one he is appointed to face. Within its own frame the argument is exact — and it is only scaffolding for the deeper architecture coming at 2.39.
verses.connection: Brightens 2.31: not merely permitted, the righteous fight is — within kshatriya-dharma — a privilege chance has handed him.
verses.reflection: Recognize one open door in your life right now that struggle disguised as misfortune.
verses.background: The teaching descends from metaphysics to station: Krishna reminds Arjuna of his own dharma as a kshatriya — the open door of righteous battle, the infamy of retreat, what his peers and enemies will say — and seals it with the verse of equanimity that makes fighting sinless.

verses.devanagari:
अथ चेत्त्वमिमं धर्म्यं संग्रामं न करिष्यसि ।
ततः स्वधर्मं कीर्तिं च हित्वा पापमवाप्स्यसि॥
verses.transliteration:
atha cettvamimaṃ dharmyaṃ saṃgrāmaṃ na kariṣyasi
tataḥ svadharmaṃ kīrtiṃ ca hitvā pāpamavāpsyasi
verses.essence: But if you will not wage this righteous war, then, abandoning your dharma and your honor, you will incur sin.
verses.translation: But if thou wilt not fight this righteous war, then having abandoned thine own duty and fame, thou shalt incur sin.

anvaya.meaning: abandoning your own dharma and fame

verses.explanation: The mirror argument: Arjuna has assumed that *fighting* is the sin and withdrawal the purity. Krishna inverts it — for the one person charged with protecting dharma, walking away *is* the sin, an abandonment (hitvā) of post. There is no neutral square on this board: the protector's inaction is itself an action, with consequences he will own. Pacifism is honorable for the renunciate who has left all stations; it is desertion for the soldier standing at his. Arjuna wanted to refuse the choice; Krishna shows him the refusal is also a choice, and the worse one.
verses.connection: The shadow of 2.32: the same dharma that makes the fight a privilege makes flight a transgression.
verses.reflection: Calculate honestly what avoiding your duty is already costing — avoidance has a price sheet too.
verses.background: The teaching descends from metaphysics to station: Krishna reminds Arjuna of his own dharma as a kshatriya — the open door of righteous battle, the infamy of retreat, what his peers and enemies will say — and seals it with the verse of equanimity that makes fighting sinless.

verses.devanagari:
अकीर्तिं चापि भूतानि कथयिष्यन्ति तेऽव्ययाम् ।
सम्भावितस्य चाकीर्तिर्मरणादतिरिच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
akīrtiṃ cāpi bhūtāni kathayiṣyanti te'vyayām
sambhāvitasya cākīrtirmaraṇādatiricyate
verses.essence: And people will recount your undying infamy — and for the honored, disgrace is worse than death.
verses.translation: People, too, will recount thy everlasting dishonour; and to one who has been honoured, dishonour is worse than death.
verses.explanation: Krishna descends another step, to the most human register: reputation. The world will not read Arjuna's heart; it will read his retreat, and tell of it forever — akīrti avyayā, an *imperishable* infamy, the dark twin of the imperishable self. The irony is deliberate: refuse the teaching about what cannot die, and you will acquire the other thing that cannot die. For a sambhāvita — one held high in honor — such disgrace outweighs death itself. This is not the Gita's loftiest argument and is not meant to be; a complete teacher addresses the whole man, including the part that cares what the world will say.
verses.connection: Extends 2.33 from inner sin to outer name: dharma abandoned has a social afterlife, and it does not fade.
verses.reflection: Care a little less today what the story says, and more what the deed actually was.
verses.background: The teaching descends from metaphysics to station: Krishna reminds Arjuna of his own dharma as a kshatriya — the open door of righteous battle, the infamy of retreat, what his peers and enemies will say — and seals it with the verse of equanimity that makes fighting sinless.

verses.devanagari:
भयाद्रणादुपरतं मंस्यन्ते त्वां महारथाः ।
येषां च त्वं बहुमतो भूत्वा यास्यसि लाघवम्॥
verses.transliteration:
bhayādraṇāduparataṃ maṃsyante tvāṃ mahārathāḥ
yeṣāṃ ca tvaṃ bahumato bhūtvā yāsyasi lāghavam
verses.essence: The great warriors will think you fled the battle from fear — and you, whom they held in highest regard, will fall into their contempt.
verses.translation: The great car-warriors will think that thou hast withdrawn from the battle through fear; and thou wilt be lightly held by them who have thought much of thee.

anvaya.meaning: as withdrawn from battle out of fear
and you, having been highly esteemed by them

verses.explanation: The cruelest precision: Arjuna believes his withdrawal would be read as compassion; Krishna names how it will actually be read — bhayāt, *from fear*. The mahārathas on both sides — Karna, Duryodhana, men who have envied his standing for decades — will never credit a crisis of conscience; cowardice is the only story they are equipped to tell. His weightiness (bahumata, much-esteemed) will turn to lāghava, lightness — the awful physics of reputation, where gravity once lost is not regained. The world cannot see motives; it can only see exits.
verses.connection: Sharpens 2.34 from anonymous report to named audience: the very peers whose regard built Arjuna will author his disgrace.
verses.reflection: The people who respect your strength are watching how you carry this — carry it upright.
verses.background: The teaching descends from metaphysics to station: Krishna reminds Arjuna of his own dharma as a kshatriya — the open door of righteous battle, the infamy of retreat, what his peers and enemies will say — and seals it with the verse of equanimity that makes fighting sinless.

verses.devanagari:
अवाच्यवादांश्च बहून्वदिष्यन्ति तवाहिताः ।
निन्दन्तस्तव सामर्थ्यं ततो दुःखतरं नु किम्॥
verses.transliteration:
avācyavādāṃśca bahūnvadiṣyanti tavāhitāḥ
nindantastava sāmarthyaṃ tato duḥkhataraṃ nu kim
verses.essence: Your enemies will speak many unspeakable words, mocking your prowess. What could be more painful than that?
verses.translation: Thy enemies also, cavilling at thy power, will speak many abusive words. What is more painful than this?

anvaya.meaning: what indeed is more painful than that

verses.explanation: The argument-from-honor reaches its floor: avācya-vādān, words that should not be spoken — taunts coarse beyond repeating — aimed precisely at his sāmarthya, the warrior's competence that is the core of his self-image. Krishna ends with a question rather than a claim: *you* tell me what is more painful. He is making Arjuna run the comparison himself — the imagined agony of fighting kin against the guaranteed agony of a lifetime of mockery. The honor arguments (2.33–36) now stand complete; whether or not they are the *highest* reasons, they are true predictions, and Arjuna knows it.
verses.connection: Bottom of the descent begun at 2.34: from history's verdict, to peers' contempt, to enemies' open mockery.
verses.reflection: Imagine your critics narrating your retreat; then act so their version becomes unwritable.
verses.background: The teaching descends from metaphysics to station: Krishna reminds Arjuna of his own dharma as a kshatriya — the open door of righteous battle, the infamy of retreat, what his peers and enemies will say — and seals it with the verse of equanimity that makes fighting sinless.

verses.devanagari:
हतो वा प्राप्स्यसि स्वर्गं जित्वा वा भोक्ष्यसे महीम् ।
तस्मादुत्तिष्ठ कौन्तेय युद्धाय कृतनिश्चयः॥
verses.transliteration:
hato vā prāpsyasi svargaṃ jitvā vā bhokṣyase mahīm
tasmāduttiṣṭha kaunteya yuddhāya kṛtaniścayaḥ
verses.essence: Slain, you gain heaven; victorious, you enjoy the earth. Therefore arise, Kaunteya, resolved to fight.
verses.translation: Slain, thou wilt obtain heaven; victorious, thou wilt enjoy the earth; therefore, stand up, O son of Kunti, resolved to fight.
verses.explanation: The warrior-logic closes with a perfect dilemma turned inside out: Arjuna saw two losses (win and lose family, lose and lose all); Krishna shows two gains — death in righteous battle opens heaven, victory yields the earth. Within kshatriya-dharma the accounting is flawless: the downside has been deleted. And then the command repeats from 2.3 — uttiṣṭha, *stand up* — but now buttressed by every level of argument from metaphysics to honor to outcome. Note what Krishna has quietly done: met Arjuna's either/or despair with an either/or hope. The frame itself — acting *for* outcomes — still stands. The next verse will be the first crack in that frame.
verses.connection: Completes the conventional case begun at 2.31: by the warrior's own arithmetic, both branches of the battle now pay.
verses.reflection: Reframe one risk today as Krishna does: count what is gained in both outcomes, then move.
verses.background: The teaching descends from metaphysics to station: Krishna reminds Arjuna of his own dharma as a kshatriya — the open door of righteous battle, the infamy of retreat, what his peers and enemies will say — and seals it with the verse of equanimity that makes fighting sinless.

verses.devanagari:
सुखदुःखे समे कृत्वा लाभालाभौ जयाजयौ ।
ततो युद्धाय युज्यस्व नैवं पापमवाप्स्यसि॥
verses.transliteration:
sukhaduḥkhe same kṛtvā lābhālābhau jayājayau
tato yuddhāya yujyasva naivaṃ pāpamavāpsyasi
verses.essence: Holding pleasure and pain, gain and loss, victory and defeat as equal — then join the battle. Thus you will incur no sin.
verses.translation: Having made pleasure and pain, gain and loss, victory and defeat the same, engage thou in battle for the sake of battle; thus thou shalt not incur sin.

anvaya.meaning: thus you will not incur sin

verses.explanation: In one verse, the door to everything that follows. Until now: fight, because heaven or earth will pay you (2.37). Suddenly: make the payments *equal* — same kṛtvā — pleasure-pain, gain-loss, victory-defeat — and *then* fight. The motivational engine of 2.37 is dismantled one verse after it was built. What remains when outcomes no longer drive the act? Pure duty, performed because it is one's own, with the result released in advance. And here is the answer to Arjuna's deepest terror — sin: it is not the act that binds, but the craving wound into it. An act done in equanimity, Krishna says, leaves no stain. The word yujyasva — *yoke yourself* — quietly plants the seed of the word 'yoga' one verse before its formal arrival. Commentators mark this as the Gita's first complete teaching — equanimity as the precondition of right action — delivered before any metaphysics of soul or God. That ordering is the method: the poem secures the stance first, then supplies the worldviews that can hold it. Sukha-duhkhe same kritva is quoted as the whole yoga in four words.
verses.connection: The hinge of the chapter: outcome-logic (2.31–37) is suddenly transcended, preparing the explicit turn to buddhi-yoga in 2.39.
verses.reflection: Before your next contest, vow: equal in win and loss — then enter, and watch fear shrink.
verses.background: The teaching descends from metaphysics to station: Krishna reminds Arjuna of his own dharma as a kshatriya — the open door of righteous battle, the infamy of retreat, what his peers and enemies will say — and seals it with the verse of equanimity that makes fighting sinless.

verses.devanagari:
एषा तेऽभिहिता साङ्ख्ये बुद्धिर्योगे त्विमां शृणु ।
बुद्ध्या युक्तो यया पार्थ कर्मबन्धं प्रहास्यसि॥
verses.transliteration:
eṣā te'bhihitā sāṅkhye buddhiryoge tvimāṃ śṛṇu
buddhyā yukto yayā pārtha karmabandhaṃ prahāsyasi
verses.essence: This wisdom of Sankhya has been declared to you; now hear it in Yoga — yoked to which, Partha, you will cast off the bondage of karma.
verses.translation: This, which has been taught to thee, is wisdon concerning Sankhya. Now listen to wisdom concerning Yoga, endowed with which, O Arjuna, thou shalt cast off the bonds of action.

anvaya.meaning: this has been declared to you
but hear this now in Yoga
you will cast off the bondage of karma

verses.explanation: Krishna names what he has done and what he is about to do. Everything from 2.11 to 2.30 was sāṅkhya — the discriminative knowledge of what is real. Now comes yoga — the same wisdom turned into a *method*, a way of acting. The promise is precise: karma-bandhaṁ prahāsyasi, you will shed the bondage of action. This is the exact answer to Arjuna's paralysis, for his real fear was never battle but *bondage* — that action would chain him to sin. Krishna's reply: action does not bind; acting *for the fruit* binds. There exists a way of doing in which the doing leaves no residue. The rest of the Gita is the unfolding of this single sentence.
verses.connection: Names the transition that 2.38 enacted: from knowing the deathless self to a discipline of unbound action.
verses.schools_note: 'Sankhya' and 'yoga' here predate the later philosophical schools: Shankara reads them as knowledge versus action-discipline; others as theory and practice of one teaching. The promise 'karma-bandham prahasyasi' — you will cast off the bondage of karma — is read by all as buddhi-yoga's defining power: the same acts, unbinding.
verses.reflection: You have understood enough theory; ask now for the discipline that makes understanding walk.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
नेहाभिक्रमनाशोऽस्ति प्रत्यवायो न विद्यते ।
स्वल्पमप्यस्य धर्मस्य त्रायते महतो भयात्॥
verses.transliteration:
nehābhikramanāśo'sti pratyavāyo na vidyate
svalpamapyasya dharmasya trāyate mahato bhayāt
verses.essence: In this path no effort is lost, no reversal occurs. Even a little of this dharma rescues from great fear.
verses.translation: In this there is no loss of effort, nor is there any harm (production of contrary results or transgression). Even a little of this knowledge (even a little practice of this Yoga) protects one from great fear.

anvaya.meaning: is there loss of effort begun

verses.explanation: Before teaching the method, Krishna posts its guarantee — written against the two fine-print clauses of Vedic ritual, where a rite abandoned midway is wasted (abhikrama-nāśa) and a rite done wrong backfires (pratyavāya). Karma-yoga has neither clause. Every step counts; no step harms; there is no wrong way to begin and no penalty for imperfection. Svalpam api — even a sliver — protects from mahato bhayāt, the great fear: the existential dread Arjuna is drowning in at this very moment. It is the most encouraging sentence in the chapter: this path cannot be failed, only paused. Svalpam apy asya dharmasya trayate mahato bhayat — even a little of this dharma rescues from great fear. The tradition treasures the verse as the spiritual economy's kindest clause: no minimum investment, no forfeiture, every attempt banked. Madhusudana calls it the seeker's insurance against the terror of starting over.
verses.connection: Underwrites the new path of 2.39: unlike ritual religion, this yoga is risk-free and cumulative — every attempt is banked.
verses.reflection: Begin the smallest practice today knowing it cannot be wasted — this path keeps every deposit.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिरेकेह कुरुनन्दन ।
बहुशाखा ह्यनन्ताश्च बुद्धयोऽव्यवसायिनाम्॥
verses.transliteration:
vyavasāyātmikā buddhirekeha kurunandana
bahuśākhā hyanantāśca buddhayo'vyavasāyinām
verses.essence: Here the resolute understanding is one, Kurunandana. Many-branched and endless are the thoughts of the irresolute.
verses.translation: Here, O joy of the Kurus, there is but a single one-pointed determination; many-branched and endless are the thoughts of the irresolute.
verses.explanation: The first technical requirement: vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ — an understanding with the nature of settled resolve, gathered to a single point. Its opposite is drawn from Arjuna's own state: thoughts bahu-śākhāḥ, many-branched — duty pointing one way, love another, fear of sin a third, fear of shame a fourth — an inner committee in permanent session. The mind fragmented across aims cannot act cleanly; the mind unified can. Karma-yoga's first gift is the collapse of the committee: one criterion (is this mine to do?) replaces the endless audit of outcomes. Singleness, not cleverness, is the entry fee.
verses.connection: Begins specifying the buddhi of 2.39: before all else, it must be *one* — the precise cure for the branching paralysis of chapter 1.
verses.reflection: Gather your scattered wantings for one hour behind a single resolve, and feel the power of one.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
यामिमां पुष्पितां वाचं प्रवदन्त्यविपश्चितः ।
वेदवादरताः पार्थ नान्यदस्तीति वादिनः॥
verses.transliteration:
yāmimāṃ puṣpitāṃ vācaṃ pravadantyavipaścitaḥ
vedavādaratāḥ pārtha nānyadastīti vādinaḥ
verses.essence: The undiscerning, delighting in the letter of the Veda, Partha, utter flowery speech, declaring: there is nothing else.
verses.translation: Flowery speech is uttered by the unwise, taking pleasure in the eulogising words of the Vedas, O Arjuna, saying, "There is nothing else."

anvaya.meaning: delighting in the words of the Veda

verses.explanation: Now the chief enemy of one-pointedness is named, and the target is audacious: not vice, but a certain *religion*. The aviapaścitaḥ — undiscerning — who delight in veda-vāda, the letter of scripture, preaching puṣpitā vāc, blossoming speech: gorgeous ritual promises of heaven, cattle, sons, rain. Their creed is nānyad asti — 'there is nothing else,' nothing beyond the marketplace of rites and rewards. Krishna's point is not anti-Veda (he will honor it at 2.46) but anti-reduction: when religion becomes a transaction catalogue, it multiplies desires instead of dissolving them — and a desire multiplied is a mind branched.
verses.connection: Diagnoses the social source of the many-branched mind of 2.41: an entire religious culture organized around desirable fruits.
verses.reflection: Notice the flowery promises you still believe — the ones that keep you shopping instead of arriving.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
कामात्मानः स्वर्गपरा जन्मकर्मफलप्रदाम् ।
क्रियाविशेषबहुलां भोगैश्वर्यगतिं प्रति॥
verses.transliteration:
kāmātmānaḥ svargaparā janmakarmaphalapradām
kriyāviśeṣabahulāṃ bhogaiśvaryagatiṃ prati
verses.essence: Souls of desire, bent on heaven, they offer speech rich in rites that yield birth as the fruit of works — aimed at enjoyment and power.
verses.translation: Full of desires, having heaven as their goal, (they utter speech which is directed to ends) leading to new births as the result of their works, and prescribe various methods abounding in specific actions, for the attainment of pleasure and power.

anvaya.meaning: those whose very self is desire
(speech) that yields rebirth as the fruit of action
aimed at the goal of enjoyment and lordship

verses.explanation: The portrait sharpens into a single devastating compound: kāmātmānaḥ — those whose *self is made of desire*, identity and craving fused. Their ceiling is svarga, heaven imagined as pleasure perfected; their instrument, an abundance of special rites; their actual harvest — and here is Krishna's quiet bombshell — janma, *rebirth*. The ritual economy they trust does not free its customers; it recycles them. Even its successes return as new bodies with new appetites. A heaven that is the fruit of works is, by that very fact, temporary — spiritual interest payments, never the principal.
verses.connection: Continues 2.42: behind the flowery speech, a psychology (desire-as-self) and its inescapable revolving door (rebirth).
verses.reflection: Trace one desire to its actual destination: is it a place, or just a better-decorated wheel?
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
भोगैश्वर्यप्रसक्तानां तयापहृतचेतसाम् ।
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिः समाधौ न विधीयते॥
verses.transliteration:
bhogaiśvaryaprasaktānāṃ tayāpahṛtacetasām
vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ samādhau na vidhīyate
verses.essence: For those clinging to enjoyment and power, their minds carried off by that speech, the resolute understanding does not arise in samadhi.
verses.translation: For those who are attached to pleasure and power, whose minds are drawn away by such teaching, ï1thatï1 determinate reason is not formed which is steadily bent on meditation and Samadhi (superconscious state).

anvaya.meaning: of those attached to enjoyment and power
whose minds are carried away by that (speech)

verses.explanation: The indictment closes by naming the casualty. Apahṛta-cetasām — minds *stolen*, carried off like plunder by beautiful promises. And what the theft prevents: vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ... samādhau — the one-pointed understanding can find no footing in samādhi, the settled depth of the mind. The sequence is mechanical, not moralistic: attachment to outcomes → mind hijacked by whatever advertises outcomes → no inner stillness possible. Arjuna can now see his own chapter-one collapse in this mirror: a mind seized by imagined fruits (kingdom, sin, family ruin) and therefore incapable of standing in one place.
verses.connection: Completes the arc 2.41–44: why the many-branched mind happens — fruit-fixation steals the very faculty that yoga requires.
verses.reflection: Find what your attachments have made you unable to sit still for — there is your homework.
