verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
त्रैगुण्यविषया वेदा निस्त्रैगुण्यो भवार्जुन ।
निर्द्वन्द्वो नित्यसत्त्वस्थो निर्योगक्षेम आत्मवान्॥
verses.transliteration:
traiguṇyaviṣayā vedā nistraiguṇyo bhavārjuna
nirdvandvo nityasattvastho niryogakṣema ātmavān
verses.essence: The Vedas deal with the three gunas — be beyond the three gunas, Arjuna: free of the pairs, ever in sattva, beyond acquiring and keeping, established in the Self.
verses.translation: The Vedas deal with the three attributes (of Nature); be thou above these three attributes. O Arjuna, free yourself from the pairs of opposites, and ever remain in the ality of Sattva (goodness), freed from (the thought of) acisition and preservation, and be established in the Self.

anvaya.meaning: the Vedas have the three gunas as their scope
free from the pairs of opposites

verses.explanation: The boldest sentence yet: the Vedas themselves operate within traiguṇya — the three strands of nature, sattva, rajas, tamas, the whole machinery of the conditioned world — and Arjuna is told to step *beyond* the machinery: nistraiguṇyo bhava. Then four marks of the one who has: free of the dvandvas, the pairs (hot-cold, gain-loss) that whipsaw the mind; resting in steady clarity; nir-yogakṣema — released from the two anxieties that run ordinary life, *getting* what one lacks and *guarding* what one has; and ātmavān, self-possessed in the deepest sense. It is a portrait of the unhooked human being.
verses.connection: Raises 2.42–44 to its conclusion: not merely avoid bad religion — outgrow the entire fruit-bearing order the rites belong to.
verses.schools_note: Commentators soften the apparent anti-Vedism: Shankara reads it as transcending the *ritual portion* (karma-kāṇḍa) of the Veda, whose authority holds for the desirous but is fulfilled and surpassed in knowledge — not as rejecting the Veda's own highest teaching.
verses.reflection: Step outside one pair of opposites today — gain-loss, praise-blame — and breathe the unpaired air.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
यावानर्थ उदपाने सर्वतः सम्प्लुतोदके ।
तावान्सर्वेषु वेदेषु ब्राह्मणस्य विजानतः॥
verses.transliteration:
yāvānartha udapāne sarvataḥ samplutodake
tāvānsarveṣu vedeṣu brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ
verses.essence: As much use as a well when water floods on every side — that much use are all the Vedas to a brahmana who knows.
verses.translation: To the Brahmana who has known the Self, all the Vedas are of as much use as is a reservoir of water in a place where there is a flood.

anvaya.meaning: as much purpose as in a well
when water overflows on all sides
for the brahmana who truly knows

verses.explanation: A flood-image resolves the tension of the last four verses with perfect courtesy. A well is not condemned when the flood comes — it was real water, honestly useful, in the time of scarcity. But to a man standing in water to the horizon, the well is simply *included*. So with the rites and rewards of scripture for the vijānat, the one who knows: nothing is denounced, everything is exceeded. Every finite goal the rituals offer is contained in the infinite the knower stands in. This is how the Gita transcends — never by burning the ladder, only by reaching the roof.
verses.connection: The peace treaty after 2.42–45: ritual religion is not false but small — a well honored, then engulfed, by the flood of knowledge.
verses.reflection: Once the well is everywhere, stop guarding your old buckets; let a smaller method retire gracefully.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन ।
मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि॥
verses.transliteration:
karmaṇyevādhikāraste mā phaleṣu kadācana
mā karmaphalaheturbhūrmā te saṅgo'stvakarmaṇi
verses.essence: Your right is to action alone, never to its fruits. Be not the author of the fruits of action; be not attached to inaction.
verses.translation: Thy right is to work only, but never with its fruits; let not the fruits of action be thy motive, nor let thy attachment be to inaction.

anvaya.meaning: be not the cause (motivated by) the fruits of action
nor let your attachment be to inaction

verses.explanation: The most famous sentence in the Gita, and a fourfold precision instrument. One: your adhikāra — jurisdiction, rightful domain — covers the action itself, the only thing actually in your hands. Two: it never covered the fruits, which are settled by a web of causes no agent controls; claiming them is claiming territory you do not own. Three: do not make fruit the *motor* of the act — work driven by reward is work owned by anxiety. Four — the clause that seals Arjuna's last exit — do not therefore slide into loving *inaction*: renouncing work to escape its results is just fruit-fixation wearing robes. What remains is astonishingly clean: full engagement in the deed, full release of the outcome. The archer owns the loosing of the arrow; the wind owns the rest. The most quoted Sanskrit sentence in the modern world, and its four clauses are read as four fences: a right to action, none to its fruits; no authoring of outcomes; no escape into inaction. Tilak built his karma-yoga politics on it; Gandhi called it his daily compass. Its genius is what it leaves untouched: effort itself, fully commanded.
verses.connection: Compresses the entire teaching since 2.39 into one rule of life — and shuts the door (clause four) on the very retreat Arjuna proposed in chapter 1.
verses.schools_note: Each tradition holds this verse as charter: Shankara as preparation for knowledge (action purifies, then knowing frees); Ramanuja as lifelong worship through duty; Tilak's Karmayoga-shastra as the gospel of engaged action; Gandhi called it the Gita in miniature. The clause all guard: 'ma te sango 'stv akarmani' — detachment must never decay into inaction.
verses.reflection: Do one task today with full craft and zero claim — your right is to the work alone.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
योगस्थः कुरु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा धनञ्जय ।
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योः समो भूत्वा समत्वं योग उच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
yogasthaḥ kuru karmāṇi saṅgaṃ tyaktvā dhanañjaya
siddhyasiddhyoḥ samo bhūtvā samatvaṃ yoga ucyate
verses.essence: Established in yoga, perform actions, abandoning attachment, Dhananjaya — equal in success and failure. Equanimity is called yoga.
verses.translation: Perform action, O Arjuna, being steadfast in Yoga, abandoning attachment and balanced in success and failure. Evenness of mind is called Yoga.

anvaya.meaning: being equal in success and failure

verses.explanation: And then, the definition — the first of the Gita's great one-line definitions of yoga: samatvaṁ yoga ucyate. Not posture, not breath, not trance: *evenness* is yoga. The verse choreographs the whole stance — stand in the yoked state, *act* (kuru karmāṇi: the command is to do, not to abstain), drop the clinging, stay level through siddhi and asiddhi alike. Evenness is not indifference; the archer still aims with total care. It is the refusal to let the self rise and fall with the arrow's luck. Spoken to 'Dhananjaya', the famous *winner of wealth*, the name itself becomes the lesson: the great winner is now taught the wealth of not needing to win. Samatvam yoga ucyate — the Gita's first formal definition of its title word. Not union, not trance: evenness. Commentators note the definition is given mid-instruction about work, fixing yoga's native habitat in the active life; the seated disciplines of chapter 6 will be its training ground, not its address.
verses.connection: States 2.47 positively and crowns it with a definition: what releasing the fruits *produces* in a person is samatva — and that evenness is the whole of yoga.
verses.reflection: Define yoga for yourself this hour: steadiness while acting, evenness while outcomes land.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
दूरेण ह्यवरं कर्म बुद्धियोगाद्धनञ्जय ।
बुद्धौ शरणमन्विच्छ कृपणाः फलहेतवः॥
verses.transliteration:
dūreṇa hyavaraṃ karma buddhiyogāddhanañjaya
buddhau śaraṇamanviccha kṛpaṇāḥ phalahetavaḥ
verses.essence: Far inferior is mere action to the yoga of understanding, Dhananjaya. Take refuge in understanding — pitiable are those driven by fruits.
verses.translation: Far lower than the Yoga of wisdon is action, O Arjuna. Seek thou refuge in wisdom; wretched are they whose motive is the fruit.
verses.explanation: A ranking, and then a hard word. Action driven by results stands far below action carried by buddhi-yoga — the same deed, transformed by the consciousness performing it. Then the word the Upanishads reserve for the deepest poverty: kṛpaṇa, the pitiable miser. Why miserly? Because the fruit-driven trade the infinite for the finite — they spend a deathless self chasing perishable returns, the worst exchange rate in existence. The remedy is stated as shelter, not technique: buddhau śaraṇam anviccha — take *refuge* in understanding, live from it as from a roof.
verses.connection: Ranks what 2.47–48 distinguished: same outward work, but the inner yoga is the higher thing — and fruit-hunger is spiritual poverty.
verses.reflection: Take refuge in understanding today, not in outcomes — pity the bargainer you were yesterday.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
बुद्धियुक्तो जहातीह उभे सुकृतदुष्कृते ।
तस्माद्योगाय युज्यस्व योगः कर्मसु कौशलम्॥
verses.transliteration:
buddhiyukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛtaduṣkṛte
tasmādyogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam
verses.essence: One yoked to understanding leaves behind, even here, both good and evil deeds. Therefore yoke yourself to yoga: yoga is skill in action.
verses.translation: Endowed with wisdom (evenness of mind), one casts off in this life both good and evil deeds; therefore, devote thyself to Yoga; Yoga is skill in action.
verses.explanation: Two thunderclaps in one verse. First: the buddhi-yukta drops *both* sukṛta and duṣkṛta — not evil only, but the whole credit-and-debit ledger of karma. Merit binds as surely as sin (golden chains are chains); freedom is not a high score but the end of scoring, and it happens *iha*, here, not after death. Second, the chapter's second great definition: yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam — yoga is skill in action. The supreme skill is not technical excellence but this: to act with everything and be bound by nothing — work so clean it leaves no residue on the worker. Arjuna feared action as a trap; Krishna reveals action, rightly done, as the art form of the free. Yogah karmasu kaushalam — yoga is skill in action — became the motto of countless institutions, and the commentaries guard it from misreading: the skill is not technical polish but the art of acting without self-poisoning, buddhi-yoked, fruit-released. Excellence, on this reading, is a byproduct of inner cleanliness.
verses.connection: Answers Arjuna's terror of sin from 1.36–45 at the root: the yoga of 2.48–49 dissolves the binding power of *all* deeds, white and black alike.
verses.reflection: Bring skill to one action today as worship: yogah karmasu kaushalam — excellence as offering.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
कर्मजं बुद्धियुक्ता हि फलं त्यक्त्वा मनीषिणः ।
जन्मबन्धविनिर्मुक्ताः पदं गच्छन्त्यनामयम्॥
verses.transliteration:
karmajaṃ buddhiyuktā hi phalaṃ tyaktvā manīṣiṇaḥ
janmabandhavinirmuktāḥ padaṃ gacchantyanāmayam
verses.essence: The wise, yoked to understanding, abandoning the fruit born of action, freed from the bondage of birth, go to the state beyond sorrow.
verses.translation: The wise, possessed of knowledge, having abandoned the fruits of their actions, and being freed from the fetters of birth, go to the place which is beyond all evil.

anvaya.meaning: released from the bondage of birth

verses.explanation: The full trajectory in one sweep: release the fruit → the bond of rebirth unties → arrival at the padam anāmayam, the place without sickness, where nothing aches. The promise answers Arjuna's opening wound with anatomical precision — his grief 'dried up the senses' (2.8), an affliction nothing attainable could cure; here is the unafflicted state, reached not by gaining anything but by *releasing* one thing. The mechanics are exact: what chains a being to the wheel is not action but the hook of expectation inside it. Unhook the fruit, and the wheel has nothing left to grip.
verses.connection: Extends 2.50 to its horizon: the ledger closed in this life becomes, at death, freedom from the wheel itself.
verses.reflection: Imagine working a whole week with no thirst for its fruits — taste the freedom in the thought.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
यदा ते मोहकलिलं बुद्धिर्व्यतितरिष्यति ।
तदा गन्तासि निर्वेदं श्रोतव्यस्य श्रुतस्य च॥
verses.transliteration:
yadā te mohakalilaṃ buddhirvyatitariṣyati
tadā gantāsi nirvedaṃ śrotavyasya śrutasya ca
verses.essence: When your understanding crosses beyond the thicket of delusion, then you will reach indifference to what has been heard and what is yet to be heard.
verses.translation: When thy intellect crosses beyond the mire of delusion, then thou shalt attain to indifference as to what has been heard and what has yet to be heard.

anvaya.meaning: to what is yet to be heard and what has been heard

verses.explanation: A milestone is set, with a striking marker. Moha-kalila — delusion as a thicket, a mire, exactly the tangle Arjuna named at 2.7 ('my mind is confused about dharma'). Crossed, it yields nirveda toward śrotavya and śruta — disinterest in all the doctrines yet to be heard and already heard. Scripture-weariness as a sign of *progress*: when the destination is seen, the appetite for more maps quietly dies. The mind stops collecting teachings the way a man who has found water stops collecting descriptions of wells (the flood-image of 2.46 still working underneath).
verses.connection: Begins answering 'how will I know it's working?' — first sign: the compulsive hunger for more answers, more authority, subsides.
verses.reflection: Notice one inherited confusion you have already crossed — the far bank exists; you have touched it.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.devanagari:
श्रुतिविप्रतिपन्ना ते यदा स्थास्यति निश्चला ।
समाधावचला बुद्धिस्तदा योगमवाप्स्यसि॥
verses.transliteration:
śrutivipratipannā te yadā sthāsyati niścalā
samādhāvacalā buddhistadā yogamavāpsyasi
verses.essence: When your understanding, bewildered by the scriptures, stands unmoving and steady in samadhi — then you will attain yoga.
verses.translation: When thy intellect, which is perplexed by the Veda text, which thou hast read, shall stand immovable and steady in the Self, then thou shalt attain Self-realisation.
verses.explanation: The second milestone, and the destination named. Śruti-vipratipannā — a mind whipsawed by revelation itself, hearing the tradition command war here and ahimsa there (Arjuna's exact predicament). Yoga arrives when that buffeted understanding comes to rest: niścalā, acalā — motionless, unwavering — in samādhi, the gathered depth. Not when the contradictions are resolved on paper, but when the mind no longer needs them resolved to be still. The criterion of arrival is not knowledge but *stillness* — and with that word the teaching pauses, because Arjuna is about to ask his first real question since surrendering: what does such a person actually look like?
verses.connection: Completes the pair begun at 2.52 and lands on 'samadhi' — the word that triggers Arjuna's great question in the next verse.
verses.reflection: Let your mind stand still amid contradictory advice today, unbribed by any of it.
verses.background: Krishna names a second wisdom: buddhi-yoga, the discipline of understanding in action. Standing in the war-chariot he teaches the right to work without claim on fruits, evenness as yoga, and skill in action — counsel for crossing beyond the bewilderment of all doctrines.

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
स्थितप्रज्ञस्य का भाषा समाधिस्थस्य केशव ।
स्थितधीः किं प्रभाषेत किमासीत व्रजेत किम्॥
verses.transliteration:
sthitaprajñasya kā bhāṣā samādhisthasya keśava
sthitadhīḥ kiṃ prabhāṣeta kimāsīta vrajeta kim
verses.essence: Arjuna asks: What marks the one of steady wisdom, who abides in samadhi, Keshava? How does the steady-minded speak, how sit, how walk?
verses.translation: What, O Krishna, is the description of him who has steady wisdom, and is merged in the superconscious state? How does one of steady wisdom speak, how does he sit, how does he walk?

anvaya.meaning: of the one of steady wisdom

verses.explanation: Arjuna speaks for the first time since na yotsye (2.9) — and the change in him is the real news. No argument, no lament: a question, and the *right* question. Not 'is this true?' but 'what does it look like when lived?' — kiṁ prabhāṣeta, kim āsīta, vrajeta kim: how does such a one speak, sit, move? He is asking for a portrait, not a proof — the saint described at street level. The tradition treasures this instinct: truth must be visible in a gait, audible in a sentence, or it is only philosophy. The rest of the chapter (2.55–72) is Krishna's answer — the Gita's first full portrait of the liberated human being.
verses.connection: The word 'samadhi' in 2.53 becomes a doorway: the student, fully a student now, asks to *see* the goal in human form.
verses.reflection: Ask of every ideal you admire: how does it walk, sit, speak? Demand the lived version.
verses.background: Arjuna asks his first question of the teaching itself — no longer about the war but about the goal: how does the person of steady wisdom speak, sit, walk? He asks for the portrait of the state being described.

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
प्रजहाति यदा कामान्सर्वान्पार्थ मनोगतान् ।
आत्मन्येवात्मना तुष्टः स्थितप्रज्ञस्तदोच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
prajahāti yadā kāmānsarvānpārtha manogatān
ātmanyevātmanā tuṣṭaḥ sthitaprajñastadocyate
verses.essence: When one fully casts off all desires of the mind, Partha, satisfied in the Self by the Self alone — then one is called steady in wisdom.
verses.translation: When a man completely casts off, O Arjuna, all the desires of the mind and is satisfied in the Self by the Self, then is he said to be one of steady wisdom.

anvaya.meaning: then one is called steady in wisdom

verses.explanation: Krishna begins the portrait from the inside — the one mark no observer can fake-check, stated first because everything visible follows from it. Prajahāti kāmān sarvān mano-gatān: drops *all* the desires that live in the mind. And the question every hearer asks — what fills the vacancy? — is answered in the same breath: ātmany evātmanā tuṣṭaḥ, content in the Self, *by* the Self — satisfaction that needs no supply line to the world. Ordinary contentment is a transaction; this is a property. Desires are not suppressed by force; they are outgrown by a fullness that arrived from within. The lamp does not crave other lights. Atmany evatmana tushtah — satisfied in the self, by the self: the tradition hears the economy of liberation in the doubled word. Every other satisfaction is imported; this one is domestic. Shankara notes the sthita-prajna portrait begins with subtraction — desires gone — because the native contentment needs no installation, only uncovering.
verses.connection: The portrait's foundation: before describing how the sage speaks or walks (2.54), name the inner state that makes the walk what it is.
verses.reflection: Find one desire you could release today and discover the self contenting itself by itself.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
दुःखेष्वनुद्विग्नमनाः सुखेषु विगतस्पृहः ।
वीतरागभयक्रोधः स्थितधीर्मुनिरुच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
duḥkheṣvanudvignamanāḥ sukheṣu vigataspṛhaḥ
vītarāgabhayakrodhaḥ sthitadhīrmunirucyate
verses.essence: Unshaken in sorrows, without craving in joys, free of passion, fear and anger — that one is called a sage of steady mind.
verses.translation: He whose mind is not shaken by adversity, who does not hanker after pleasures, and is free from attachment, fear and anger, is called a sage of steady wisdom.

anvaya.meaning: free from passion, fear and anger
is called a sage of steady mind

verses.explanation: Now the weather test. Sorrows arrive — the mind does not convulse (anudvigna-manāḥ: un-shaken, not un-feeling); joys arrive — no grasping after more of them, no clinging as they pass. Then the triple release that makes this possible: rāga, the pull toward; bhaya, the recoil from what threatens; krodha, the burn when the pull is blocked. These three are one mechanism at different temperatures — and all three are children of dependency on outcomes. Withdraw the dependency (2.55's inner fullness) and the mechanism goes quiet. The sage still stands in the rain and the sun; what is gone is the inner scrambling for shelter.
verses.connection: Tests 2.55's fullness against the two weathers, pain and pleasure — equanimity now shown as lived temperament, not theory.
verses.reflection: In the next small upset, watch for the muni's mark: no spike of craving, fear, or anger.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
यः सर्वत्रानभिस्नेहस्तत्तत्प्राप्य शुभाशुभम् ।
नाभिनन्दति न द्वेष्टि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता॥
verses.transliteration:
yaḥ sarvatrānabhisnehastattatprāpya śubhāśubham
nābhinandati na dveṣṭi tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā
verses.essence: One without attachment on any side, who meeting good or evil neither rejoices nor recoils — that one's wisdom stands firm.
verses.translation: He who is everywhere without attachment, on meeting with anything good or bad, who neither rejoices not hastes, his wisdom is fixed.
verses.explanation: The refrain that will toll through the rest of the chapter sounds for the first time: tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā — that one's wisdom is *installed*, load-bearing, built on rock. The mark here is subtle: anabhisneha is not coldness but the absence of sneha in its root sense — the *sticky oil* of attachment, the film that makes experiences adhere to the self. Events still arrive, śubha and aśubha, welcome and unwelcome; the sage neither throws a parade nor slams a door. Things pass through such a person the way light passes through clear glass — fully received, nothing retained as residue.
verses.connection: Generalizes 2.56 from great sorrows and joys to the whole stream of daily this-and-that — and coins the chapter's refrain.
verses.reflection: Receive today's good news and bad news with the same face, just once, as an experiment.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
यदा संहरते चायं कूर्मोऽङ्गानीव सर्वशः ।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता॥
verses.transliteration:
yadā saṃharate cāyaṃ kūrmo'ṅgānīva sarvaśaḥ
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyastasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā
verses.essence: And when, like a tortoise drawing in its limbs, one withdraws the senses from their objects on every side — that one's wisdom stands firm.
verses.translation: When, like the tortoise which withdraws on all sides its limbs, he withdraws his senses from the sense-objects, then his wisdom becomes steady.
verses.explanation: The portrait's first image, and a masterpiece of selection: the tortoise. When the moment threatens, it draws in its limbs — saṁharate, gathers home — not by cutting them off but by *retracting* them, whole and alive, beneath the shell. So the sage with the five senses: not blinded, not deafened, but capable of recall — attention answers to its owner. The choice of animal does half the teaching: nothing slower, calmer, longer-lived, or less aggressive than a tortoise; withdrawal here is unhurried sovereignty, not fear. And limbs drawn in can be extended again — the senses remain available servants; they have simply ceased to be kidnappers.
verses.connection: From inner state (2.55–57) to the body's gates: the first *practice* visible in the sage — command over where attention goes.
verses.reflection: Practice the tortoise once: feel a provocation, and draw the senses in instead of out.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
विषया विनिवर्तन्ते निराहारस्य देहिनः ।
रसवर्जं रसोऽप्यस्य परं दृष्ट्वा निवर्तते॥
verses.transliteration:
viṣayā vinivartante nirāhārasya dehinaḥ
rasavarjaṃ raso'pyasya paraṃ dṛṣṭvā nivartate
verses.essence: Sense-objects fall away from the abstinent embodied one — all but the taste. Even the taste departs when the Supreme is seen.
verses.translation: The objects of the senses turn away from the abstinent man leaving the longing (behind); but his longing also turns away on seeing the Supreme.

anvaya.meaning: even the taste, for this one

verses.explanation: The most psychologically honest verse in the chapter. Abstain, and the *objects* recede — but rasa remains: the taste, the remembered relish, the inner tongue still flavoring what the mouth renounced. Every dieter, every reformed addict, every ascetic knows this residue; willpower can starve the behavior but not the savor, and a craving merely caged is a craving waiting. The cure is not more force but a *better taste*: paraṁ dṛṣṭvā — when the Supreme is actually seen, tasted, the lesser rasa falls away on its own, the way a child's sweets lose their kingdom the day something finer is known. Renunciation, the Gita insists, is completed not by subtraction but by encounter.
verses.connection: The honest footnote to 2.58: retraction controls exposure, not appetite — only the sight of the Higher dissolves the root-relish.
verses.schools_note: Bhakti traditions hear in 'paraṁ dṛṣṭvā' their whole theology: the senses are not enemies to be starved but lovers to be re-aimed; Ramanuja reads the 'Supreme seen' as the Lord himself, whose sweetness retires all others.
verses.reflection: Be patient with cravings that fade but still whisper — only a higher taste retires them fully.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
यततो ह्यपि कौन्तेय पुरुषस्य विपश्चितः ।
इन्द्रियाणि प्रमाथीनि हरन्ति प्रसभं मनः॥
verses.transliteration:
yatato hyapi kaunteya puruṣasya vipaścitaḥ
indriyāṇi pramāthīni haranti prasabhaṃ manaḥ
verses.essence: For even of the striving, discerning man, Kaunteya, the turbulent senses carry off the mind by force.
verses.translation: The turbulent senses, O Arjuna, do violently carry away the mind of a wise man though he be striving (to control them).

anvaya.meaning: carry away the mind by force

verses.explanation: Lest the portrait breed complacency, a storm warning. Even the yatat, the one actively striving, even the vipaścit, the discerning — the senses remain pramāthīni, churning, riot-prone, and they take the mind prasabham, *by force*, the verb of abduction. No achievement retires the risk; vigilance is not a phase of the path but its permanent climate. There is deep kindness folded into the severity: if the senses can storm even the wise, then Arjuna's own past defeats are not proof of worthlessness — they are the standard weather of embodiment, to be answered with watchfulness rather than shame.
verses.connection: Counterweight to 2.58–59: the tortoise's skill is real but never automatic — the chapter refuses to let mastery sound easy.
verses.reflection: Respect the senses' strength today; the wise are wary precisely because they know the pull.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
तानि सर्वाणि संयम्य युक्त आसीत मत्परः ।
वशे हि यस्येन्द्रियाणि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता॥
verses.transliteration:
tāni sarvāṇi saṃyamya yukta āsīta matparaḥ
vaśe hi yasyendriyāṇi tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā
verses.essence: Restraining them all, one should sit yoked, intent on Me. For whose senses are mastered, that one's wisdom stands firm.
verses.translation: Having restrained them all he should sit steadfast, intent on Me; his wisdom is steady whose senses are under control.

anvaya.meaning: intent on Me, holding Me supreme
for whose senses are under control

verses.explanation: Into a discourse that has been austerely impersonal — self, gunas, buddhi — falls one small bomb of a word: mat-paraḥ, *intent on Me*. The first 'Me' of the Gita's teaching voice. The logic is practical before it is theological: the mind cannot hold a vacuum; senses restrained from objects must be given somewhere *better* to rest, and an abstraction makes a poor resting place. A person — the divine charioteer two feet away — can be loved, and love concentrates as discipline alone never will. Here, in a single compound, the entire bhakti of chapters 7–12 sends its first green shoot above the soil.
verses.connection: Supplies what 2.60's warning demands: restraint alone is unstable — the senses need not a cage but a Center, and Krishna quietly offers himself.
verses.schools_note: 'Mat-parah' — intent on Me — is the first personal-devotional anchor inside the sthita-prajna teaching, and the schools weight it differently: Shankara glosses it as intent on the Self (Vasudeva as inner Self); Ramanuja reads it as the doctrine that sense-control is impossible without taking refuge in the Lord — discipline borrowed from devotion.
verses.reflection: Anchor the senses in something higher before the storm, not during it — sit for two minutes now.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
ध्यायतो विषयान्पुंसः सङ्गस्तेषूपजायते ।
सङ्गात्सञ्जायते कामः कामात्क्रोधोऽभिजायते॥
verses.transliteration:
dhyāyato viṣayānpuṃsaḥ saṅgasteṣūpajāyate
saṅgātsañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmātkrodho'bhijāyate
verses.essence: Brooding on sense-objects, attachment to them is born; from attachment springs desire; from desire, anger is born.
verses.translation: When a man thinks of the objects, attachment for them arises; from attachment desire is born; from desire anger arises.
verses.explanation: Now the autopsy of a fall — the Gita's famous causal chain, and its first link is the masterstroke: not contact with objects but dhyāyataḥ, *brooding* on them. Ruin begins as meditation pointed the wrong way; the mind circling an object is already worshiping it. Repetition warms thought into saṅga (the stickiness of 2.57 returning), saṅga ferments into kāma — desire with a deadline — and desire, the moment the world declines to cooperate, ignites as krodha. Anger is desire's grief. Note what this chain quietly absolves: the object itself. The mango is innocent; the *rehearsal* of the mango is where the avalanche starts. The most precise psychology in the poem: dhyayato vishayan — it begins with mere dwelling, attention parked on an object. Commentators emphasize that the chain has no first crime, only a first carelessness; sanga, kama, krodha follow as compounding interest. The defense, therefore, is attentional, not moral: guard where the mind parks.
verses.connection: Explains the abduction of 2.60 frame by frame: how the senses' coup actually unfolds — beginning not at the gates but in the throne room of attention.
verses.reflection: Catch one thought today at the brooding stage — before it becomes desire, then anger, then ruin.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
क्रोधाद्भवति सम्मोहः सम्मोहात्स्मृतिविभ्रमः ।
स्मृतिभ्रंशाद् बुद्धिनाशो बुद्धिनाशात्प्रणश्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
krodhādbhavati sammohaḥ sammohātsmṛtivibhramaḥ
smṛtibhraṃśād buddhināśo buddhināśātpraṇaśyati
verses.essence: From anger comes delusion; from delusion, loss of memory; from lost memory, the ruin of understanding — and when understanding is ruined, one is lost.
verses.translation: From anger comes delusion; from delusion loss of memory; from loss of memory the destruction of discrimination; from the destruction of discrimination he perishes.

anvaya.meaning: from broken memory, destruction of understanding
from the ruin of understanding, one perishes

verses.explanation: The chain's second half, where psychology becomes free fall. Anger thickens into sammoha — the red fog in which the world distorts; the fog dissolves smṛti — not data-memory but *remembrance*: who I am, what I learned, what I vowed (every furious person forgets themselves first). With remembrance gone, buddhi — the faculty this whole chapter has labored to build — collapses; and with buddhi gone, praṇaśyati: the person is destroyed, no arrow required. Eight links from a daydream to a ruin. The chain is the chapter's dark mirror — and its mercy is its precision: a sequence this exact can be cut, and the scissors work best at link one. The cascade's hinge is smriti-bhramsha — the shattering of memory: in anger one forgets who one is and what one knows, and from that amnesia the discrimination dies. The tradition reads the Gita's final verse-pair as this chain's reversal: smritir labdha, memory regained (18.73). The whole poem runs between a forgetting and a remembering.
verses.connection: Completes 2.62's avalanche to the bottom — Arjuna's own chapter-one collapse, traced in slow motion, now legible as a sequence rather than a fate.
verses.reflection: Memorize the chain: anger, delusion, broken memory, lost discernment — and guard the first link.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
रागद्वेषविमुक्तैस्तु विषयानिन्द्रियैश्चरन् ।
आत्मवश्यैर्विधेयात्मा प्रसादमधिगच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
rāgadveṣavimuktaistu viṣayānindriyaiścaran
orviyuktaistu ātmavaśyairvidheyātmā prasādamadhigacchati
verses.essence: But the self-governed one, moving among objects with senses freed from craving and aversion, held in the Self's command — attains serenity.
verses.translation: But the self-controlled man, moving among the objects with the senses under restraint and free from attraction and repulsion, attains to peace.

anvaya.meaning: but with (senses) freed from craving and aversion
moving among objects with the senses

verses.explanation: The counter-picture, and a crucial correction of scope: caran viṣayān — *moving among objects*. The free person is not the one who fled the marketplace but the one who walks through it with senses ātma-vaśya, answering to their owner, scrubbed of the twin magnets rāga and dveṣa. Same shops, same colors, same noise; nothing in the scene has hooks anymore. The prize is named prasāda — a beautiful double word: serenity, and the *grace-offering* returned from a shrine. Tranquility, the verse hints, is what the world hands back to the one who stopped grabbing at it.
verses.connection: Answers 2.62–63 point for point: the same senses, the same objects — but with the magnets removed, the avalanche has nothing to start from.
verses.reflection: Move through the same world today minus the clinging, and notice serenity arriving uninvited.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
प्रसादे सर्वदुःखानां हानिरस्योपजायते ।
प्रसन्नचेतसो ह्याशु बुद्धिः पर्यवतिष्ठते॥
verses.transliteration:
prasāde sarvaduḥkhānāṃ hānirasyopajāyate
prasannacetaso hyāśu buddhiḥ paryavatiṣṭhate
verses.essence: In serenity is born the end of all sorrows; for the clear-hearted, the understanding quickly stands firm.
verses.translation: In that peace all pains are destroyed; for the intellect of the tranil-minded soon becomes steady.
verses.explanation: What serenity is *for*. In prasāda, sorrows lose their purchase — sarva-duḥkhānāṁ hāniḥ, the waning of all of them — because suffering's real habitat was never events but the turbid mind that received them. And then a word of tempo, rare in this patient chapter: āśu, *swiftly*. In a clear heart, wisdom settles fast — the way sediment needs only stillness, not effort, to sink. All the chapter's labor (restraint, refuge, evenness) turns out to be the making of stillness; once the water is calm, clarity is not achieved but simply *happens*.
verses.connection: Pays out 2.64's prasāda: serenity is not the path's decoration but its engine — the condition in which the steady buddhi finally takes hold by itself.
verses.reflection: When the heart clears even briefly, watch how quickly the intellect steadies — peace works fast.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
नास्ति बुद्धिरयुक्तस्य न चायुक्तस्य भावना ।
न चाभावयतः शान्तिरशान्तस्य कुतः सुखम्॥
verses.transliteration:
nāsti buddhirayuktasya na cāyuktasya bhāvanā
na cābhāvayataḥ śāntiraśāntasya kutaḥ sukham
verses.essence: For the unyoked there is no understanding, nor contemplation; without contemplation, no peace — and without peace, where is happiness?
verses.translation: There is no knowledge of the Self to the unsteady and to the unsteady no meditation is possible, and to the unmeditative there can be no peace, and to the man who has no peace, how can there be happiness?

anvaya.meaning: there is no understanding for the unyoked
and no peace for the uncontemplative
for the peaceless, where is happiness

verses.explanation: The same staircase, descended. The ayukta — unyoked, the mind scattered across its branches (2.41 returning) — has no steady buddhi; without it, no bhāvanā, the dwelling-power of sustained attention; without dwelling, no śānti; and then the question that ends every hedonist's argument before it starts: aśāntasya kutaḥ sukham — for the peaceless, *where* would happiness even live? The unanswerable rhetorical question is the point: pleasure poured into a churning mind is water poured into a cracked pot. Arjuna sought a formula for happiness in chapter one — kingdom? family? — and here is the audit: every route runs through peace, and peace runs through the yoked mind. There is no detour.
verses.connection: The negative image of 2.64–65: the four-step ladder shown from below, proving there is no path to sukha that bypasses the gathered buddhi.
verses.reflection: No peace without focus, no happiness without peace — trace today's restlessness back up this chain.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
इन्द्रियाणां हि चरतां यन्मनोऽनुविधीयते ।
तदस्य हरति प्रज्ञां वायुर्नावमिवाम्भसि॥
verses.transliteration:
indriyāṇāṃ hi caratāṃ yanmano'nuvidhīyate
tadasya harati prajñāṃ vāyurnāvamivāmbhasi
verses.essence: For the mind that follows the roving senses carries off wisdom, as wind carries a ship on the waters.
verses.translation: For the mind, which follows in the wake of the wandering senses, carries away his discrimination, as the wind (carries away) a boat on the waters.

anvaya.meaning: that carries away this one's wisdom
as wind a ship on the waters

verses.explanation: The chapter's nautical image, perfectly engineered. A ship has rudder, cargo, destination; the wind has none — only force and whim. Let the mind anuvidhīyate, *follow along after* whichever sense is gusting, and the whole vessel of wisdom is dragged off course; one open sail is enough — the verse says the mind following the senses (plural becoming singular), a single indulged channel suffices for the hijack. The image dignifies even as it warns: you are not driftwood, you are a *ship* — built for crossing, equipped to steer. Wreckage is not fate; it is helmsmanship declined.
verses.connection: Compresses the chains of 2.62–63 and the ladder of 2.66 into one picture: the mind's allegiance — rudder or wind — decides everything.
verses.reflection: Mind one wandering sense today like a helmsman minds wind — one gust can take the whole ship.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
तस्माद्यस्य महाबाहो निगृहीतानि सर्वशः ।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता॥
verses.transliteration:
tasmādyasya mahābāho nigṛhītāni sarvaśaḥ
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyastasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā
verses.essence: Therefore, mighty-armed one, whose senses are wholly withheld from their objects — that one's wisdom stands firm.
verses.translation: Therefore, O mighty-armed Arjuna, his knowledge is steady whose senses are completely restrained from sense-objects.
verses.explanation: The tasmāt — *therefore* — that gathers the whole excursion (2.60–67) and closes the ring: the verse all but repeats 2.58, and the refrain prajñā pratiṣṭhitā tolls for the last time. But the same words return heavier. At 2.58 the tortoise was an introduction; now it arrives proven — through the storm warning, the anatomy of the fall, the counter-picture, the ship. Mahābāho, mighty-armed: Krishna salutes the warrior's strength while relocating it — the arms that drew Gandiva are now invited to a harder pull, the inward one. Scripture repeats, the tradition says, when it knows the first hearing was only a hearing.
verses.connection: Closes the loop opened at 2.58: the tortoise-teaching restated as a verdict, now carrying the full weight of everything between.
verses.reflection: Withdraw deliberately from one stimulation today, and notice wisdom getting room to stand.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
या निशा सर्वभूतानां तस्यां जागर्ति संयमी ।
यस्यां जाग्रति भूतानि सा निशा पश्यतो मुनेः॥
verses.transliteration:
yā niśā sarvabhūtānāṃ tasyāṃ jāgarti saṃyamī
yasyāṃ jāgrati bhūtāni sā niśā paśyato muneḥ
verses.essence: What is night for all beings, there the restrained one is awake; where beings wake, that is night for the seeing sage.
verses.translation: That which is night to all beings, in that the self-controlled man is awake; when all beings are awake, that is night for the Muni (sage) who sees.

anvaya.meaning: what is night for all beings
in that the restrained one is awake

verses.explanation: The most haunting verse of the chapter — a chiasmus of two inverted days. The reality in which the sage is wide awake (the Self, the changeless) is pitch night to the world: invisible, unsuspected, slept through. The reality in which the world conducts its blazing noon — profit, rank, fear, prize — is night to the muni: he sees it, but as one sees in the dark, knowing the shapes for shadows. Neither party is *blind*; each is asleep precisely where the other lives. It is the gentlest possible statement of an immense claim: the sage and the world are not on one spectrum of more and less wisdom — they are facing opposite ways. To begin this path is to accept a reversed clock. Ya nisha sarva-bhutanam — the verse of inverted days. Commentators read it without contempt: the sage is not insomniac among sleepers but differently awake; what the world cannot see, he lives in, and what the world chases, he sleeps through. It is the gentlest statement of the price of clarity: a certain solitude of schedule.
verses.connection: After the practice (2.58–68), the strangeness: a quiet warning and a deep comfort — the steadied one will be a foreigner in the world's daylight.
verses.schools_note: The night-and-day inversion is read two ways: Advaita as the epistemic reversal — what the world calls real, the seer sees through; the devotional schools as a portrait of the contemplative's solitude. Madhusudana adds the warning gloss: the verse describes realized vision, not a license for the seeker to dismiss the world's daylight duties.
verses.reflection: What the world calls necessary may be your night; where you wake may look like sleep to them.

verses.devanagari:
आपूर्यमाणमचलप्रतिष्ठं
समुद्रमापः प्रविशन्ति यद्वत् ।
तद्वत्कामा यं प्रविशन्ति सर्वे
स शान्तिमाप्नोति न कामकामी॥
verses.transliteration:
āpūryamāṇamacalapratiṣṭhaṃ samudramāpaḥ praviśanti yadvat
tadvatkāmā yaṃ praviśanti sarve sa śāntimāpnoti na kāmakāmī
verses.essence: As waters enter the ocean — ever filled, unmoving in its bed — so all desires enter such a one, who attains peace; not the desirer of desires.
verses.translation: He attains peace into whom all desires enter as waters enter the ocean which, filled from all sides, remains unmoved; but not the man who is full of desires.

anvaya.meaning: as the waters enter the ocean

verses.explanation: The chapter's crowning image. Rivers pour into the ocean continuously — yet it neither floods (āpūryamāṇam: full, yet receiving) nor demands them (it sends no summons to the rivers); its level holds because its fullness is its own. So with the sage: stimuli and desires still *enter* — praviśanti, the verse is frank, the sage is no sealed vault — but they enter a vastness already complete, and vanish without raising the waterline. Against this stands the kāma-kāmī, the desirer of desires, a creek in drought begging every cloud. The difference is not what flows in; it is the size of what receives. Peace, finally defined, is fullness so established that nothing arriving can add to it — and therefore nothing withheld can wound it. The ocean simile carries a fine print the commentaries love: apuryamanam — the sea is constantly being filled, and constantly full. The sage is not the one rivers avoid; everything still flows in. Achala-pratishtham: the level does not rise. Peace, on this image, is not the absence of input but the presence of depth.
verses.connection: Resolves the chapter's central question — how to live amid stimulation (2.64) — with its definitive image: not the dam, but the sea.
verses.reflection: Be the ocean once today: let something flow into you without raising your level.
verses.background: Krishna answers with the sthita-prajna portrait, the close of the chapter the tradition calls the Gita in miniature: desires released, the tortoise-withdrawal of senses, the chain from brooding to ruin, the ocean-like peace — ending with the Brahman-state that holds even at the hour of death.

verses.devanagari:
विहाय कामान्यः सर्वान्पुमांश्चरति निःस्पृहः ।
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः स शान्तिमधिगच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
vihāya kāmānyaḥ sarvānpumāṃścarati niḥspṛhaḥ
nirmamo nirahaṅkāraḥ sa śāntimadhigacchati
verses.essence: The one who abandons all desires and moves free of longing, without 'mine', without 'I' — that one attains peace.
verses.translation: That man attains peace who, abandoning all desires, moves about without longing, without the sense of mine and without egoism.
verses.explanation: The ocean-image translated into a way of walking: carati, he *moves about* — in the world, on its roads — stripped of three things, each subtler than the last. Niḥspṛhaḥ, no reaching; nirmamaḥ, no 'mine' — and the chapter's first arrow was māmakāḥ, *my* people (1.1): the war itself began in that pronoun; finally nirahaṅkāraḥ, no 'I'-maker — the root from which every 'mine' ever grew. The sequence is an excavation: behavior, then possession, then the possessor. What remains when even the claimant is surrendered? Not a void — the verse answers with its verb: peace, śānti, walking around on two feet.
verses.connection: Brings 2.70's ocean ashore: the same fullness as a human gait — and quietly closes the wound of 1.1 by dissolving its pronoun.
verses.reflection: Drop the words 'I' and 'mine' for one hour and watch how many burdens were grammatical.

verses.devanagari:
एषा ब्राह्मी स्थितिः पार्थ नैनां प्राप्य विमुह्यति ।
स्थित्वास्यामन्तकालेऽपि ब्रह्मनिर्वाणमृच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
eṣā brāhmī sthitiḥ pārtha naināṃ prāpya vimuhyati
sthitvāsyāmantakāle'pi brahmanirvāṇamṛcchati
verses.essence: This is the state of Brahman, Partha. Attaining it, one is deluded no more. Established in it even at the hour of death, one reaches the nirvana of Brahman.
verses.translation: This is the Brahmic seat (eternal state), O son of Pritha. Attaining to this, none is deluded. Being established therein, even at the end of life, one attains to oneness with Brahman.

anvaya.meaning: attaining this, one is deluded no more
one attains the nirvana of Brahman

verses.explanation: The chapter sets its seal. Everything since 2.55 — the contentment, the tortoise, the ocean — is now given its true name: brāhmī sthitiḥ, the standing-in-Brahman, a *state*, not an experience that visits and departs. Its first property answers the chapter's first diagnosis: na vimuhyati — no relapse into the moha that felled Arjuna at 1.28. Its last property answers his deepest terror: held anta-kāle api, even in death's hour, it opens into brahma-nirvāṇa — extinction not of the self but of the fever, the blowing-out of every flame that burned. The teaching that began with a man collapsed between two armies ends with a word for peace beyond extinguishing. The argument of the Gita is, in one sense, complete — what remains, sixteen chapters of it, is Arjuna learning to ask: but how do I *live* this? Brahmi sthitih — the Brahman-state — and the clause tradition leans on: api ante, even at the end, even at the last hour. Shankara reads it as proof that liberation needs no minimum tenure; Ramanuja as the door grace keeps open. The chapter that began with a collapse closes with a state no collapse can revoke.
verses.connection: Names and seals the whole portrait (2.55–71) — and answers chapter one's three wounds: delusion, paralysis, and the fear of death.
verses.schools_note: 'Brahma-nirvana' — extinguishing into Brahman — is rare vocabulary shared with Buddhist idiom, and commentators noticed: Shankara reads it as dissolution of the false self into the real; theistic schools as the soul's entry into Brahman's life, not its erasure. 'Api ante' — even at the last hour — all schools read as the door that never closes.
verses.reflection: Hold the brahmi sthiti as your evening thought: a state one can reach even at the last hour.
